proposals.title: Dynamic Fugacity-Based Modeling of Bioconcentration Factors (BCFs) for Organic Chemicals Under Pulsed and Fluctuating Aqueous Exposures
proposals.motivation: Environmental risk assessments for organic chemicals have traditionally relied on steady-state models (e.g., standard BCF tests) that assume constant exposure concentrations. Real-world aquatic environments, however, are characterized by fluctuating or pulsed chemical exposures due to episodic discharges, rainfall events, or tidal cycles. This mismatch may lead to significant errors in predicting bioaccumulation and internal dose, which is critical for accurate PBT (Persistence, Bioaccumulation, Toxicity) evaluation.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that for low-volatility, hydrophobic organic chemicals, the kinetic delay in bioconcentration caused by fluctuating aqueous concentrations can be systematically predicted and will deviate significantly from steady-state predictions, with the magnitude of deviation governed by chemical fugacity capacity and organism-specific uptake/elimination rate constants.
proposals.method: We will develop a dynamic, non-steady-state fugacity model building on Mackay's previous work. The model will use a time-variable input function for aqueous concentration (e.g., sinusoidal or pulsed patterns) coupled to a one-compartment organism model. Key chemical properties (e.g., Kow, Koa) will define fugacity capacities and partition coefficients. We will numerically solve the resulting differential equations to track internal fugacity and concentration over time. The approach will be validated against existing time-series bioaccumulation data for SVOCs (e.g., certain PCBs, siloxanes) and applied to simulate realistic exposure scenarios.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a quantitative framework for moving beyond the steady-state assumption in bioaccumulation assessment, yielding time-dependent BCFs. It will directly inform environmental modeling and risk assessment by highlighting conditions where steady-state models fail, thereby improving the accuracy of internal dose estimation for toxicity under realistic exposure regimes.
proposals.novelty: While fugacity modeling is well-established, its explicit application to model bioconcentration under deliberately non-steady-state, fluctuating aqueous exposures represents a novel synthesis. It integrates the concepts of kinetic delay in partitioning (from Mackay's dust/air work) and equifinality/uncertainty analysis into the dynamic bioaccumulation context.

proposals.title: Integrating Metabolic Transformation Kinetics into a Fugacity-Based Uptake-Release Model for Bioaccumulation Assessment of Organic Chemicals
proposals.motivation: Current fugacity-based bioaccumulation models, while powerful for simulating the partitioning and transport of organic chemicals, often treat organisms as passive compartments or use simplistic, constant elimination rates. This fails to capture the critical role of metabolic transformation, a dominant loss process for many bioaccumulative organic compounds, leading to significant uncertainty in predictions of internal concentrations and toxicity. Incorporating mechanistic metabolic kinetics is essential for improving the reliability of PBT (Persistence, Bioaccumulation, Toxicity) assessments and chemical management.
proposals.hypothesis: Explicitly incorporating Michaelis-Menten-type metabolic transformation kinetics into a dynamic fugacity-based bioaccumulation model will significantly improve the prediction of steady-state body burdens and time-to-elimination for metabolizable organic chemicals, reducing the uncertainty and equifinality observed in current model calibrations.
proposals.method: We will extend an existing fugacity-based uptake-release model (e.g., as used in MacKay's prior work) by adding a metabolizing sub-compartment within the organism module. The transformation rate will be governed by Michaelis-Menten kinetics, parameterized by chemical-specific in vitro hepatic clearance data (Vmax, Km) from pre-2026 literature (e.g., for PAHs, flame retardants). The model will be implemented numerically, solving the system of differential equations for fugacity (or activity) in water, organism lipids, and the metabolic sink. We will calibrate and validate the extended model using time-series bioaccumulation data for chemicals with known metabolic pathways.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a more mechanistically rigorous modeling framework for bioaccumulation assessment that explicitly separates physical-chemical partitioning from biochemical transformation. It will yield a practical tool for extrapolating in vitro metabolism data to predict in vivo bioaccumulation kinetics, directly supporting higher-tier PBT evaluations. The model will also help quantify the impact of metabolic variability on overall exposure and risk.
proposals.novelty: While metabolism is acknowledged in bioaccumulation science, its integration into dynamic, multi-media fugacity models as a kinetically controlled process, rather than a fixed elimination rate, is not standard. This approach directly links the model's internal chemical activity to biochemical parameters, moving beyond the conventional use of octanol-water partition coefficients (Kow) as the sole predictor of bioaccumulation potential for metabolizable organics.

proposals.title: Quantifying Mass Transfer Resistances Across the Hydrophobicity Spectrum: A Fugacity-Based Model Evaluation for Extreme KOW Chemicals
proposals.motivation: Environmental fate models for organic chemicals, particularly those based on fugacity and activity, rely on accurate parameterizations of intermedia mass transfer resistances. Pre-2026 knowledge indicates these resistances are often assumed to be inversely related to hydrophobicity (log KOW), but this relationship is poorly quantified for chemicals at the extremes of the hydrophobicity range (e.g., log KOW < 1 or > 9). This gap introduces significant uncertainty in predicting bioaccumulation, long-range transport, and overall persistence (PBT) for a wide array of emerging contaminants, from highly hydrophilic pharmaceuticals to super-hydrophobic polymers and siloxanes.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the dependence of air-water, particle-water, and membrane-water mass transfer resistances on chemical hydrophobicity is non-linear and plateaus or reverses at extreme log KOW values, leading to kinetic delays not captured by standard equilibrium partitioning models.
proposals.method: We will extend a well-established fugacity-based Level III/IV multimedia model, explicitly resolving diffusive and boundary-layer resistances. The model will be systematically tested across a curated chemical set spanning log KOW from -2 to 12, including per- and polyfluoroalkyl substances (PFAS), cyclic volatile methyl siloxanes (cVMS), and ionic organics. Key resistances will be parameterized using a combination of polyparameter linear free energy relationships (pp-LFERs) and the HYPHEN (Hydrophobicity-dependent PHase Exchange Numerical) algorithm, which iteratively solves for phase equilibria and kinetic limitations. Model outputs will be validated against pre-2026 experimental data on indoor dust/air partitioning kinetics and bioaccumulation studies for high-KOW chemicals.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a robust, quantitative framework for predicting environmental partitioning and transport kinetics across the entire hydrophobicity range, directly addressing equifinality issues in model calibration. It will yield a synoptic indicator of contamination potential that integrates both equilibrium and kinetic constraints, improving PBT assessments for regulatory science.
proposals.novelty: While fugacity models are common, a systematic, quantified investigation of resistance functions for extreme-hydrophobicity chemicals is lacking. This study uniquely integrates kinetic data from indoor environments (dust/air delay) with outdoor multimedia fate modeling to challenge the universality of the log KOW-resistance relationship.

proposals.title: Quantifying Bioaccumulation Kinetics: A Pharmacokinetic Compartmental Modeling Approach to Derive Lipid-Water Transfer Rate Constants for Organic Chemicals
proposals.motivation: Predicting the bioaccumulation and toxicity of organic chemicals requires understanding their kinetic transfer between environmental media and biological lipids. Traditional equilibrium-based metrics like fugacity and bioconcentration factors often fail to capture the time-dependent dynamics critical for risk assessment, especially for substances with slow kinetics or in non-steady-state scenarios. A formal kinetic framework is needed to move beyond synoptic indicators and better predict internal target-site concentrations over time.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that pharmacokinetic (PK) compartmental modeling, using time-course bioaccumulation data, can robustly estimate first-order transfer rate constants (k1, k2) between water and lipid compartments for diverse organic chemicals, and that these kinetic parameters will improve predictions of internal body burden compared to equilibrium-based models.
proposals.method: We will adapt classic one- and two-compartment PK models (e.g., mammillary models) to represent water (source) and organism lipid (central) compartments. Using existing time-series bioaccumulation data for a range of SVOCs and other organics, we will employ non-linear least-squares regression (e.g., using the `nls` function in R) to fit the models and estimate uptake (k1) and elimination (k2) rate constants. Model selection (e.g., AIC) and uncertainty analysis (e.g., Monte Carlo or Bayesian Markov Chain Monte Carlo) will be performed to address equifinality and parameter identifiability, as informed by the researcher's prior work on uncertainty.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a standardized, transparent method for deriving kinetic bioaccumulation parameters from experimental data, directly linking environmental exposure to internal dose. It will contribute a new, kinetics-focused modeling tool to the PBT assessment toolbox, complementing equilibrium fugacity approaches. The resulting rate constants will enable more accurate predictions of bioaccumulation under dynamic exposure scenarios relevant to regulatory science.
proposals.novelty: While PK modeling is standard in pharmacology, its formal application to derive fundamental inter-compartmental transfer constants for environmental organic chemicals is novel. This approach explicitly bridges the gap between the researcher's expertise in fugacity/activity concepts and the kinetic delays observed in systems like dust/air partitioning, providing a mechanistic, time-resolved alternative to equilibrium-based indicators.

proposals.title: Predicting Lipid-Phase Mass Transfer Coefficients for Organic Contaminants: A QSAR Approach to Quantify Bioaccumulation Resistance
proposals.motivation: The bioaccumulation of organic chemicals in organisms is a critical determinant of their toxicity and persistence. Current models, often reliant on equilibrium partitioning (fugacity), can be inaccurate for substances with slow kinetics, where lipid-phase resistance controls uptake rates. Pre-2026, there is a lack of predictive models for these kinetic resistance parameters, hindering accurate risk assessment for many low-volatility SVOCs and other chemicals exhibiting kinetic delay.
proposals.hypothesis: Lipid-phase mass transfer coefficients (or resistance parameters) for organic chemicals are not random but are systematically related to their molecular structure and physicochemical properties, enabling prediction via Quantitative Structure-Activity Relationship (QSAR) models.
proposals.method: We will compile a curated dataset of experimentally derived lipid-phase resistance parameters or in vivo uptake kinetics for diverse organic compounds from literature. Molecular descriptors (e.g., log K_ow, molar volume, topological indices, hydrogen-bonding capacity) will be calculated using tools like PaDEL-Descriptor or DRAGON. We will employ multiple linear regression (MLR) and machine learning algorithms (specifically Random Forest and Support Vector Regression) to develop predictive QSAR models. Model performance will be rigorously validated using OECD principles, including internal cross-validation and external test sets.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first broadly applicable QSARs for predicting kinetic resistance to lipid-phase uptake, moving beyond equilibrium assumptions. It will directly enhance bioaccumulation and toxicokinetic models, leading to more accurate predictions of chemical body burdens over time for risk assessment. The models will serve as a key tool for prioritizing chemicals with potential for kinetic bioaccumulation.
proposals.novelty: While QSARs exist for partition coefficients (e.g., log K_ow), predicting the *kinetic resistance* to partitioning into lipids is a novel application. This addresses a key uncertainty identified in multimedia fate modeling, specifically the 'kinetic delay' for SVOCs, by providing a mechanistic, descriptor-based prediction method.

proposals.title: A Fugacity-Based Model for Predicting Depuration Rates of Bioaccumulative Organic Chemicals from Aquatic Organisms to Clean Water
proposals.motivation: Risk assessment for persistent, bioaccumulative, and toxic (PBT) chemicals requires accurate prediction of depuration rates, the key process determining how quickly organisms clear contaminants after exposure ceases. Existing models often oversimplify this clearance phase by assuming instantaneous equilibrium, ignoring kinetic delays that can lead to underestimation of body burdens and exposure times. A mechanistic model focused on the depuration phase, grounded in thermodynamic principles, is needed to improve the accuracy of environmental and human health risk assessments for organic pollutants.
proposals.hypothesis: The depuration of organic chemicals from aquatic organisms into clean water can be accurately modeled using a dynamic, non-steady-state fugacity approach that explicitly accounts for kinetic limitations in chemical partitioning and clearance, providing more realistic estimates than equilibrium-based models.
proposals.method: We will develop a dynamic multi-compartment fugacity model, building on Mackay's prior work, to simulate chemical release from an organism's lipid and aqueous tissues into clean water. The model will treat the organism as a series of well-mixed compartments in disequilibrium with the external water. We will implement and solve differential equations describing mass transfer using chemical-specific fugacity capacities (Z-values) and transport parameters (D-values). The model will be parameterized and tested using existing bioaccumulation and depuration data for legacy SVOCs and cyclic volatile methyl siloxanes, with sensitivity analysis to identify key drivers of depuration kinetics.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a robust, thermodynamics-based modeling tool specifically designed for the depuration phase, directly applicable to PBT assessment and the interpretation of bioaccumulation test data. It will advance the field by quantifying the impact of kinetic delays on clearance rates, offering a more defensible scientific basis for setting environmental quality standards and remediation timelines.
proposals.novelty: While fugacity models are well-established for predicting uptake, this proposal uniquely pivots the fugacity framework to solve the 'dual problem' of clearance, explicitly modeling the non-steady-state release kinetics that are often glossed over in standard bioaccumulation assessments.

proposals.title: Deriving Water Quality Guidelines from Critical Body Residues: A Fugacity-Based Approach for Bioaccumulative Organic Chemicals
proposals.motivation: Current water quality guidelines (WQGs) based on external aqueous concentrations are problematic for bioaccumulative organic chemicals. They fail to account for variable bioavailability and the delayed toxicity driven by internal body burdens achieved through long-term exposure and bioaccumulation. A body residue-based approach directly links to the internal chemical activity causing toxicity, offering a more mechanistic and protective framework for persistent, bioaccumulative, and toxic (PBT) substances.
proposals.hypothesis: For bioaccumulative organic chemicals, water quality guidelines derived from critical body residues (CBRs) via a fugacity-based bioaccumulation model will be more protective and ecologically relevant than those based solely on external water concentrations, especially under conditions of kinetic equilibration delay.
proposals.method: The research will apply a dynamic, multi-compartment fugacity model to simulate chemical bioaccumulation in a standard aquatic organism (e.g., fish) from water and diet over time. The model, parameterized for specific organic compounds (e.g., cyclic volatile methyl siloxanes or other SVOCs), will be run to steady-state or kinetically-limited conditions. A critical body residue (CBR), expressed as an internal chemical activity or fugacity, will be used as the toxicity threshold. The model will be inverted to back-calculate the corresponding time-integrated or steady-state water concentration that would produce the CBR, thereby establishing a body residue-based WQG. Uncertainty and equifinality analysis will be conducted on key parameters (e.g., partition coefficients, uptake rates).
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, mechanistic methodology for setting WQGs that internalizes the processes of bioaccumulation and kinetic delay. It will directly bridge the gap between internal dose toxicity metrics and regulatory environmental standards. The resulting framework will offer a more synoptic indicator of contamination risk for PBT chemicals, moving beyond static water concentration limits.
proposals.novelty: While CBRs are established in toxicology, their formal integration into regulatory water quality criteria via fugacity modeling to account for kinetic equilibration delays is novel. This approach explicitly addresses the limitations of equilibrium partitioning assumptions for low-volatility SVOCs, a key focus of MacKay's recent work.

proposals.title: Thermodynamic Activity as a Unifying Dose Metric: Linking Bioaccumulation, Internal Exposure, and Baseline Toxicity for Organic Chemicals
proposals.motivation: Current chemical risk assessment often relies on external concentrations or total body burdens, which fail to account for bioavailability and the true thermodynamic potential to cause biological effects. This creates uncertainty in linking environmental exposure to internal toxicity, particularly for complex, multi-media organic pollutants like SVOCs and cyclic volatile methyl siloxanes. A fundamental, mechanistically sound dose metric is needed to unify exposure and effect assessments across environmental compartments and biological membranes.
proposals.hypothesis: Thermodynamic activity (or fugacity), rather than concentration or body burden, provides a more universal and predictive metric for baseline (narcotic) toxicity by representing the chemical's escaping tendency and effective membrane concentration. We hypothesize that internal effect concentrations for baseline toxicity across species and chemicals will converge when expressed as thermodynamic activity.
proposals.method: We will compile existing in vivo toxicity (e.g., LC50, EC50) and bioaccumulation data for a diverse set of neutral organic chemicals from pre-2026 literature. Using established polyparameter linear free energy relationships (pp-LFERs) and the COSMOtherm software, we will calculate the chemicals' activity coefficients in key biological phases (e.g., lipid, protein). Internal lethal body burdens will be converted to critical activities. The analysis will employ a Bayesian statistical framework to quantify the uncertainty and equifinality in the derived critical activity ranges, explicitly accounting for variability in chemical partitioning properties and biological response.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a rigorous, thermodynamics-based framework to predict baseline toxicity from chemical structure and multimedia exposure, reducing the need for species-specific testing. It will deliver a validated 'critical activity' threshold for narcosis, offering a synoptic indicator for environmental contamination that integrates PBT properties and directly links to potential biological impact.
proposals.novelty: While activity/fugacity is used in environmental fate modeling, its direct application as a universal internal dose metric for toxicity prediction, coupled with a formal uncertainty analysis for the derived thresholds, represents a significant conceptual and practical advance beyond conventional concentration-based approaches.

proposals.title: Non-Ideal Partitioning in Acute Contamination Scenarios: Quantifying Henry's Law Constant Deviations for High-Concentration Spills of Organic Chemicals
proposals.motivation: Environmental fate models and PBT (Persistence, Bioaccumulation, Toxicity) assessments for organic chemicals predominantly rely on equilibrium partition coefficients (e.g., Henry's law constant, H) derived from ideal, dilute laboratory systems. However, major contamination events, such as chemical spills or industrial discharges, create localized, high-concentration conditions where solute-solute and solute-solvent interactions can significantly alter chemical activity, challenging the assumption of ideality. Pre-2026, there is a critical knowledge gap in how these non-ideal conditions affect the primary air-water partitioning driver, leading to potentially large errors in predicting initial dispersion, volatilization rates, and exposure concentrations following acute releases.
proposals.hypothesis: For high-concentration spills of organic chemicals, the effective Henry's law constant will deviate significantly from its dilute-solution value due to non-ideal effects on chemical activity, and these deviations can be systematically predicted using a polyparameter linear free energy relationship (pp-LFER) framework extended with concentration-dependent activity coefficients.
proposals.method: The research will employ a two-pronged experimental and modeling approach. First, we will measure air-water partitioning for a selected set of SVOCs and ionizable organics (e.g., pharmaceuticals, pesticides) across a wide concentration range (from dilute to near saturation) using a dynamic stripping apparatus coupled with solid-phase microextraction (SPME) for headspace analysis. Second, we will model the observed deviations using the UNIFAC group contribution method to calculate concentration-dependent activity coefficients in the aqueous phase. These will be integrated into a pp-LFER model, where the solute descriptors will be regressed against the log of the measured, concentration-corrected H, creating a predictive tool for non-ideal partitioning.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first quantitative framework for correcting air-water partition coefficients under the high-concentration conditions of spills, directly enhancing the accuracy of initial-phase multimedia fate models for contamination events. It will advance the application of activity-based and fugacity approaches in non-ideal scenarios, offering a more synoptic and physically realistic indicator of environmental contamination potential immediately following a release.
proposals.novelty: While MacKay's prior work has focused on fugacity and activity in equilibrium or kinetic partitioning at ambient concentrations, this proposal explicitly targets the non-ideal, high-concentration regime—a critical but understudied boundary condition for environmental models. The integration of experimental high-concentration partitioning data with a pp-LFER framework modified for activity coefficients represents a novel synthesis of physical chemistry and environmental fate modeling.

proposals.title: Quantifying the Influence of Complex Aqueous Matrices on Partitioning Constants for Organic Contaminants: A Fugacity-Based Experimental and Modeling Study
proposals.motivation: Environmental fate models for organic pollutants, crucial for PBT (Persistence, Bioaccumulation, Toxicity) assessment, rely on partitioning constants (e.g., K_OW, K_AW) typically measured in pure water. Natural waters, however, contain dissolved salts, organic matter (DOM), and co-pollutants, which can alter chemical activity and fugacity, leading to significant errors in predicted bioaccumulation and multimedia distribution. This discrepancy introduces major uncertainty, as highlighted in recent work on equifinality in environmental models.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that for low-volatility, hydrophobic organic contaminants, the presence of salts and DOM in natural waters will systematically reduce their effective aqueous activity (increase their fugacity), thereby altering air/water and organic carbon/water partition constants compared to pure-water values, with the magnitude of change predictable from water chemistry parameters.
proposals.method: We will measure air/water (K_AW) and solid-phase microextraction/water partition constants for a suite of model SVOCs (e.g., PCBs, cyclic siloxanes) in controlled aqueous matrices: synthetic solutions with varying ionic strength (NaCl, CaCl2) and standard DOM (Suwannee River NOM). Experiments will use fugacity-based headspace and passive dosing techniques to maintain constant chemical activity, addressing kinetic equilibration delays known for SVOCs. The resulting data will be used to parameterize and test a modified polyparameter linear free energy relationship (pp-LFER) model that incorporates matrix-specific descriptors for cavity formation and solvation.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a robust, experimentally derived dataset of 'environmentally relevant' partition constants, directly reducing model uncertainty for fate and bioaccumulation predictions. It will advance the application of the fugacity/activity framework by demonstrating its necessity for accurate synoptic indicators of contamination in real ecosystems. The modified pp-LFER model will offer a practical tool for adjusting laboratory-derived constants for site-specific water quality.
proposals.novelty: While the 'salting out' effect is known, a systematic, fugacity-controlled investigation quantifying its combined effect with DOM on partitioning constants for SVOCs, explicitly linking it to PBT assessment and model equifinality, is novel. This moves beyond treating water as a passive solvent to an active, variable matrix central to accurate fate modeling.

proposals.title: Kinetic Control of Air-Water Exchange: Quantifying Mass Transfer Coefficients for Bioaccumulative Organic Chemicals in Dynamic Environmental Systems
proposals.motivation: Environmental fate and bioaccumulation assessments of organic chemicals have historically relied on equilibrium partitioning coefficients (e.g., K_AW). However, as recognized in recent work on indoor dust/air partitioning, kinetic delays can dominate the behavior of low-volatility compounds, making equilibrium assumptions invalid for dynamic systems like flowing rivers or turbulent coastal zones. A focus solely on equilibrium states may misrepresent the true exposure concentrations and bioaccumulation potential of chemicals in real-world, non-steady-state environments.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that for semi-volatile and low-volatility organic chemicals, the kinetics of air-water exchange, not the equilibrium partition coefficient, are the rate-limiting control on inter-media transport and subsequent bioaccumulation in aquatic food webs under environmentally realistic conditions.
proposals.method: We will adapt and apply a two-film resistance mass transfer model to quantify kinetic air-water exchange rates. Laboratory experiments will measure the water-side (k_w) and air-side (k_a) mass transfer coefficients for a suite of model chemicals (e.g., cyclic volatile methyl siloxanes, PCBs, current-use flame retardants) in a controlled, stirred tank system. The experimental data will be used to parameterize and validate a fugacity-based dynamic multimedia model, explicitly incorporating the calculated kinetic rates to simulate time-dependent concentrations in water and air under scenarios with variable wind speed, temperature, and water turbulence.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a robust, experimentally derived dataset of air-water mass transfer coefficients for SVOCs, filling a critical data gap. It will deliver a modeling framework that shifts the synoptic indicator of environmental contamination from a static equilibrium snapshot to a dynamic, kinetically constrained prediction, improving risk assessments for bioaccumulative chemicals in transient systems.
proposals.novelty: While the two-film model is established, its systematic experimental parameterization and integration into a fugacity-based framework specifically for SVOC bioaccumulation assessment is novel, directly inverting the traditional equilibrium focus highlighted in the researcher's prior perspective work to a kinetic paradigm.

proposals.title: A Fugacity-Based Framework for Validating and Refining Vapor Pressure and Aqueous Solubility Measurements of Low-Volatility, Bioaccumulative Organic Chemicals
proposals.motivation: Accurate vapor pressure (P_v) and aqueous solubility (S_w) data are critical for fugacity-based environmental fate models and PBT (Persistence, Bioaccumulation, Toxicity) assessments of organic chemicals. Pre-2026, significant uncertainty exists in measured data for low-volatility, superhydrophobic compounds (e.g., high MW PAHs, certain flame retardants), leading to equifinality in model predictions and unreliable synoptic indicators of contamination. This undermines regulatory decisions and risk assessments for chemicals highlighted in MacKay's prior work on cyclic volatile methyl siloxanes and SVOC partitioning.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a systematic, fugacity-based cross-validation of P_v and S_w measurement techniques for a congener series of challenging organic chemicals will reveal consistent, technique-dependent biases, enabling the development of refined experimental protocols and uncertainty bounds for these key parameters.
proposals.method: We will select a test set of 15-20 organic chemicals spanning a range of hydrophobicity (log K_ow 4-9) and low volatility (P_v < 1 Pa). For each chemical, we will perform parallel measurements using established techniques: the gas saturation method for P_v and the generator column method for S_w. Crucially, we will employ a fugacity-based closure test by using these independently measured parameters to predict indoor dust/air partition coefficients (K_dust/air) and compare them to direct, kinetically-corrected experimental measurements of K_dust/air, following MacKay's recent methodology. Discrepancies will be analyzed to identify and correct for systematic biases (e.g., sorption losses, non-equilibrium) in the P_v and S_w protocols.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a robust, internally consistent dataset of key physicochemical properties for problematic environmental chemicals, directly reducing parameter uncertainty in multimedia fate models. It will yield a validated, stepwise experimental protocol for measuring P_v and S_w of low-volatility organics, improving the reliability of fugacity-based synoptic indicators and PBT screening frameworks used by regulators and industry.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies not in inventing new instruments, but in applying a fugacity-based mass-balance closure (using kinetically-resolved indoor partition coefficients) as a diagnostic tool to validate and refine existing measurement techniques for P_v and S_w, moving beyond simple inter-laboratory comparison.

proposals.title: Validating and Refining Group Contribution Methods for Predicting Fugacity Capacities and Bioaccumulation Potential of Emerging Organic Chemicals
proposals.motivation: The rapid introduction of new organic chemicals into commerce necessitates reliable pre-market screening for persistence, bioaccumulation, and toxicity (PBT). Pre-2026, group contribution methods are widely used to estimate key partitioning constants (e.g., KOW, KOA) from molecular structure, but their accuracy for novel, complex, or low-volatility substances remains uncertain. This uncertainty undermines environmental fate models and fugacity-based synoptic indicators, creating regulatory and scientific blind spots.
proposals.hypothesis: Systematic validation against high-quality experimental data will reveal specific structural domains where existing group contribution methods fail, and that incorporating corrections for intramolecular interactions and kinetic delay factors for low-volatility SVOCs will significantly improve prediction accuracy for bioaccumulation and multimedia partitioning.
proposals.method: We will compile a curated database of experimentally determined partition coefficients and bioconcentration factors for diverse organic chemicals, prioritizing under-represented classes. We will test the predictive performance of established group contribution methods (e.g., EPI Suite, SPARC) against this dataset. Discrepancies will be analyzed to identify problematic functional group combinations and molecular geometries. We will then develop and parameterize new group interaction factors and, for semi-volatile organics (SVOCs), integrate a kinetic delay module informed by dust/air partitioning research to refine predictions of environmental fugacity capacity and bioaccumulation potential.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a validated, transparent framework for estimating critical environmental fate parameters for data-poor chemicals, directly supporting improved PBT assessment. It will advance the application of fugacity and activity-based synoptic indicators by reducing the equifinality in model outputs caused by uncertain input parameters.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the targeted integration of mechanistic insights from kinetic partitioning delays (for SVOCs in indoor environments) and uncertainty analysis into the development of next-generation group contribution methods, moving beyond simple additive schemes to address molecular complexity and non-equilibrium conditions.

proposals.title: Predicting Henry's Law Constants for Organic Pollutants: A Transferable QSPR Model Integrating Drug Discovery Descriptors and Fugacity-Based Interpretability
proposals.motivation: Henry's law constant (HLC) is a critical parameter governing the environmental fate, transport, and bioaccumulation potential of organic chemicals, yet reliable experimental data are scarce for many emerging contaminants. Accurate prediction of HLC is essential for multi-media fugacity modeling, which underpins PBT (Persistence, Bioaccumulation, Toxicity) assessments and regulatory decisions. Current quantitative structure-property relationship (QSPR) models for HLC often lack interpretability and are not optimized for the diverse chemical space of environmental pollutants.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that molecular descriptors and machine learning algorithms developed for predicting drug-like properties (e.g., solubility, partition coefficients) can be successfully transferred and adapted to create a more accurate and mechanistically interpretable QSPR model for the HLC of environmental organic chemicals.
proposals.method: We will curate a high-quality dataset of experimental HLC values for diverse organic compounds from pre-2026 literature. Molecular descriptors will be calculated using RDKit and Dragon software, focusing on 2D/3D descriptors relevant to solvation energy and volatility (e.g., topological, electronic, and quantum-chemical). We will employ and compare several machine learning algorithms, including Random Forest regression and Gradient Boosting Machines (XGBoost), implemented in scikit-learn. Model performance will be validated via rigorous cross-validation and external test sets, with feature importance analysis to link key descriptors to the underlying physical chemistry of air-water partitioning.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a robust, open-access predictive tool for HLC, directly supporting environmental fate modeling and risk assessment of data-poor chemicals. It will advance the field by explicitly linking advanced cheminformatics methods from pharmacology to environmental chemistry, offering improved predictive accuracy and mechanistic insight compared to existing group contribution or simple linear models.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the systematic adaptation of drug discovery QSPR toolkits and modern machine learning to the environmental domain, coupled with an analysis of descriptor relevance within a fugacity/activity framework to enhance model interpretability for fate modelers.

proposals.title: Inferring Field-Based Effective Henry's Law Constants for Organic Contaminants: A Fugacity and Activity-Based Inverse Modeling Approach
proposals.motivation: Henry's law constants (HLC) are critical for predicting the environmental fate, transport, and bioaccumulation potential of organic chemicals. However, laboratory-derived HLCs often fail to represent real-world conditions where complex matrices (e.g., dissolved organic matter, surfactants, non-equilibrium) in air and water alter chemical activity and fugacity. This discrepancy introduces significant uncertainty in multimedia models used for PBT (Persistence, Bioaccumulation, Toxicity) assessment and regulatory decision-making.
proposals.hypothesis: Effective Henry's law constants derived from inverse modeling of concurrent field measurements will systematically differ from laboratory pure-water values, and these differences can be explained by chemical-specific properties (e.g., hydrophobicity, volatility) and site-specific environmental factors that influence chemical activity.
proposals.method: We will compile a curated dataset of concurrent, co-located air and surface water concentration measurements for diverse organic pollutants (e.g., PCBs, PAHs, current-use pesticides, cyclic volatile methyl siloxanes) from published monitoring studies. Using a fugacity-based framework, we will formulate the inverse problem to solve for the effective HLC (H_eff = C_air / C_water). We will employ Bayesian inference to quantify the uncertainty and equifinality in H_eff estimates, explicitly accounting for measurement error and potential kinetic delays in equilibration, as informed by MacKay's prior work on indoor dust/air partitioning. The analysis will correlate deviations of H_eff from laboratory HLC with chemical descriptors and water quality parameters.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel dataset of environmentally relevant HLCs and a robust methodological framework for their derivation. It will directly quantify the 'activity gap' between laboratory and field partitioning, improving the parameterization of environmental fate models. The results will offer a synoptic, multi-media indicator of real-world chemical behavior to support more accurate risk assessments.
proposals.novelty: While forward modeling using HLCs is standard, the systematic inverse estimation of effective constants from field data to diagnose activity reductions is not routine. This approach integrates Mackay's core concepts of fugacity, activity, and model uncertainty into a targeted inverse analysis for a fundamental chemical property.

proposals.title: Fugacity-Based Modeling of the Water-to-Air Partitioning Pathway: Quantifying Kinetic Delays and Their Impact on Bioaccumulation Assessments of SVOCs
proposals.motivation: Environmental fate models for organic chemicals, particularly semi-volatile organic compounds (SVOCs), have traditionally emphasized air-to-water partitioning (e.g., gas absorption) when evaluating aquatic contamination and bioaccumulation. However, the reciprocal water-to-air pathway (e.g., volatilization from surface waters) is often implicitly treated as a simple equilibrium process, despite evidence from indoor dust/air studies showing significant kinetic delays for low-volatility compounds. This asymmetry creates uncertainty in multi-media models, potentially misrepresenting chemical fluxes, residence times, and long-range transport potential in aquatic systems.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that water-to-air partitioning for many SVOCs is subject to kinetically limited equilibration, analogous to observations in indoor environments, and that explicitly modeling this delay using a fugacity-based framework will significantly alter predictions of aquatic concentrations, overall persistence (P), and bioaccumulation (B) potential compared to conventional equilibrium assumptions.
proposals.method: We will adapt a dynamic, multi-compartment fugacity model (Level III/IV) to explicitly separate and parameterize the forward (air-to-water) and reverse (water-to-air) mass transfer coefficients (D values) for a suite of SVOCs, including cyclic volatile methyl siloxanes (cVMS) and other hydrophobic organics. The kinetic delay for the water-to-air pathway will be modeled by incorporating a system-specific resistance term, calibrated using laboratory-derived air-water exchange data and informed by the kinetic delay framework from indoor partitioning studies. Uncertainty and equifinality analysis, as applied in MacKay's prior work, will be used to identify critical parameters and evaluate model robustness.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a more symmetrical and physically realistic treatment of interfacial exchange in multi-media fate models, reducing a key source of equifinality. It will yield a novel 'synoptic indicator' that quantifies the directional bias in air-water exchange, improving the accuracy of PBT (Persistence, Bioaccumulation, Toxicity) screening for chemicals where volatilization from water is a significant loss process.
proposals.novelty: While fugacity models inherently calculate net flux, this proposal explicitly decouples and differentially constrains the two directional processes, applying the concept of kinetic delay—previously demonstrated for indoor dust/air partitioning—to the fundamental water-air interface in environmental systems.

proposals.title: Predicting Fugacity Ratios and Bioaccumulation Potentials in Complex Organic Chemical Mixtures: A Framework for Cosolvency and Competitive Sorption
proposals.motivation: Environmental risk assessment of organic chemicals typically relies on single-compartment, single-chemical fate parameters. However, real-world exposures involve complex mixtures where cosolvency can enhance apparent solubility and competitive sorption can alter partitioning, leading to inaccurate predictions of fugacity, bioaccumulation, and toxicity. Pre-2026 models lack robust, experimentally validated frameworks to predict these mixture interactions for low-volatility SVOCs across multimedia environments.
proposals.hypothesis: The fugacity capacity (Z-value) and activity coefficient of a target organic chemical in a defined environmental compartment (e.g., organic matter, indoor dust) can be predicted in complex mixtures by integrating a polyparameter linear free energy relationship (pp-LFER) framework with a competitive Langmuir-type sorption model to account for cosolvency and site competition.
proposals.method: The study will select a suite of structurally diverse, pre-2026 chemicals of concern (e.g., PAHs, phthalates, siloxanes) and measure their equilibrium partitioning in air/dust/water systems, first individually and then in defined mixtures. Mixture effects will be quantified by measuring shifts in partition coefficients (K_d) and fugacity ratios. A pp-LFER model will be parameterized from single-chemical data to describe solvation interactions. For mixtures, a competitive sorption model, implemented via iterative numerical solving in R or Python, will be calibrated to predict the observed Z-value deviations, using the mixture composition and pp-LFER descriptors as inputs.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a quantitative, mechanistic framework to predict mixture effects on key fate parameters like fugacity and bioaccumulation potential, moving beyond simple additive models. It will deliver a set of adjustable 'mixture interaction constants' that can be integrated into existing multimedia fate models (e.g., the OECD Tool) to improve risk assessments of real-world chemical exposures.
proposals.novelty: While pp-LFERs and competitive sorption are established concepts, their integration into a unified fugacity-based framework to predict non-additive mixture effects on environmental partitioning constants for SVOCs is novel and directly addresses the kinetic and equilibrium uncertainties highlighted in MacKay's prior work.

proposals.title: Fugacity-Based Modeling of Short-Lived Organic Replacement Chemicals: Quantifying Steady-State Mass and Activity in Multi-Media Environments
proposals.motivation: Regulatory pressure on persistent, bioaccumulative, and toxic (PBT) substances has driven the adoption of short-lived organic replacement chemicals (e.g., certain pharmaceuticals, modern pesticides, and high-production volume intermediates). While less persistent, their high production volumes and continuous emission can lead to 'pseudo-persistence,' creating potentially significant, yet poorly quantified, steady-state environmental burdens and toxicological exposures. Current multi-media fate models, optimized for PBT assessment, lack frameworks to efficiently evaluate the dynamic equilibrium of rapidly degrading chemicals.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a fugacity/activity-based modeling approach, when parameterized for rapid reaction kinetics and continuous emission scenarios, can reveal significant steady-state masses and activity coefficients for short-lived replacement chemicals in key environmental compartments, particularly indoor environments and water bodies receiving effluent.
proposals.method: We will adapt a Level III fugacity model, explicitly incorporating Mackay's activity-based synoptic indicator concept. The model will be parameterized for a suite of pre-2026 replacement chemicals (e.g., specific siloxane replacements, certain ionic liquids). Key innovations include: 1) implementing kinetic constraints on inter-compartmental transfer (informed by Mackay's dust/air partitioning work) to account for equilibration delays for low-volatility compounds, and 2) employing Monte Carlo uncertainty analysis (as applied to cyclic volatile methyl siloxanes) to propagate variability in degradation rate constants and emission estimates, identifying parameters controlling model equifinality for non-persistent chemicals.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel modeling framework to assess the 'pseudo-persistent' burden of short-lived chemicals, moving beyond simple half-life metrics. It will deliver quantitative estimates of steady-state concentrations, fugacities, and activity coefficients, offering a more holistic indicator of contamination potential and informing early-stage risk assessment for replacement substances before large-scale environmental accumulation is detected.
proposals.novelty: This inverts the classic PBT modeling paradigm by focusing on chemicals defined by their lack of persistence, yet potential for significant mass-in-environment due to high emission rates. It uniquely integrates Mackay's concepts of fugacity, activity, kinetic limitation, and uncertainty analysis specifically for this emerging class of contaminants.

proposals.title: A Multi-Criteria Framework for Chemical Management: Integrating Fugacity-Based Fate, Bioaccumulation, and Societal Benefit Functions
proposals.motivation: Current environmental fate and risk models for organic chemicals, such as those based on fugacity and activity, focus primarily on toxicity, persistence, and bioaccumulation (PBT) to quantify hazard. This creates a one-sided assessment that can overlook why chemicals are used in the first place. A holistic decision-support tool is needed to explicitly weigh environmental exposure against quantified societal benefits (e.g., pest control, material performance, health outcomes) to inform more nuanced regulatory and substitution strategies.
proposals.hypothesis: Explicitly integrating time-dependent benefit functions with fugacity-based multi-media fate and bioaccumulation models will identify chemical use scenarios and environmental conditions where net societal benefit is maximized for a given level of acceptable risk, moving beyond hazard-only assessments.
proposals.method: We will extend a well-established fugacity-based level III/IV model to incorporate a dynamic benefit module. For a case study chemical (e.g., a specific biocide or plasticizer), we will define benefit functions (e.g., efficacy decay, required functional concentration) parameterized from pre-2026 literature. The model will simulate simultaneous chemical distribution (air, water, soil, sediment, indoor dust) and accumulation in a generic food web over time. A Monte Carlo analysis will propagate uncertainty in physicochemical properties (as in MacKay's prior work on siloxanes) and benefit parameters. The output will be spatially explicit maps and time-series of a net benefit-risk index.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, quantitative modeling framework that bridges environmental chemistry and socio-economic analysis, offering a proof-of-concept for multi-criteria chemical assessment. It will deliver a transparent method to visualize trade-offs between benefit and environmental exposure, directly supporting the development of 'use-specific' rather than 'chemical-specific' regulations.
proposals.novelty: While fugacity models are mature for predicting fate and bioaccumulation, their direct coupling with formal, dynamic benefit quantification to calculate a net outcome is unprecedented. This moves beyond qualitative discussions of benefit to a quantitative, model-integrated approach.

proposals.title: A Global Fugacity-Based Model for Hexabromocyclododecane (HBCD): Applying the HCH Framework to Predict Long-Range Transport and Multi-Media Partitioning
proposals.motivation: Hexabromocyclododecane (HBCD), a high-production volume brominated flame retardant, is a persistent organic pollutant (POP) listed under the Stockholm Convention. Its global distribution is poorly quantified, and its environmental fate is complicated by stereoisomer-specific properties and potential for long-range transport. Predicting its global distribution is critical for informing regulatory strategies and understanding its bioaccumulation potential and toxicity in remote ecosystems.
proposals.hypothesis: The well-established fugacity-based modeling framework previously applied to hexachlorocyclohexane (HCH) can be successfully adapted to simulate the global distribution of HBCD, accurately capturing its multi-media partitioning and long-range transport patterns, provided key chemical-specific parameters (e.g., temperature-dependent partition coefficients, degradation rates) and stereoisomer differences are adequately represented.
proposals.method: We will adapt a Level III/IV fugacity-based box model, previously validated for HCH, to simulate HBCD. The model will incorporate a global compartmentalization (air, water, soil, sediment, vegetation) and use the concept of activity to determine equilibrium partitioning. Key inputs will include HBCD's physical-chemical properties (log Kow, vapor pressure, Henry's law constant), isomer-specific data, and degradation half-lives from pre-2026 literature. The modeling will explicitly address uncertainty and equifinality through Monte Carlo analysis, as per the researcher's recent work, to quantify parameter sensitivity and model robustness.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first globally comprehensive, fugacity-based synoptic model for HBCD, offering quantitative estimates of its environmental reservoirs and fluxes. It will advance the application of activity-based modeling to newer, complex POPs and deliver a validated tool for assessing the long-term environmental impact and effectiveness of global phase-out actions for HBCD.
proposals.novelty: While HCH models are mature, this represents a novel application of that proven framework to HBCD, a contaminant with distinct partitioning behavior (higher hydrophobicity, lower volatility) and complex stereochemistry. The explicit integration of uncertainty analysis and kinetic delay concepts for indoor/dust partitioning, drawn from the researcher's recent papers, will provide a more realistic and defensible global assessment.

proposals.title: Fugacity-Based Modeling of Legacy Chemical Declines: Quantifying the Time-Lagged Environmental Response to the Phase-Out of Hexachlorocyclohexane (HCH) Isomers
proposals.motivation: The global phase-out of persistent organic pollutants (POPs) like hexachlorocyclohexane (HCH) under the Stockholm Convention provides a critical natural experiment to understand the long-term dynamics of chemical decontamination. However, environmental monitoring often shows delayed declines in concentrations due to factors like secondary emissions from reservoirs and slow inter-media transport. A predictive, mechanistic understanding of these time-lagged responses is essential for validating regulatory effectiveness and forecasting future risks of legacy chemicals.
proposals.hypothesis: The post-ban decline in environmental concentrations of HCH isomers can be accurately simulated by a dynamic, multi-media fugacity model that explicitly incorporates time-variant emission scenarios, kinetic limitations in inter-media partitioning (especially for air-dust exchange), and bioaccumulation processes, with model uncertainty dominated by the characterization of these kinetic delays and reservoir release rates.
proposals.method: We will develop a region-specific, dynamic Level IV fugacity model, building on Mackay's established framework. The model will be parameterized for α-, β-, and γ-HCH using pre-2026 physicochemical properties and environmental monitoring data. Time-variant emissions will be defined by a phasedown function based on international ban dates. We will explicitly integrate a kinetic mass-transfer sub-model for indoor dust/air partitioning to account for equilibration delays for semi-volatile organic compounds (SVOCs). Model calibration and uncertainty analysis will be performed using a formal Bayesian approach or GLUE (Generalized Likelihood Uncertainty Estimation), as referenced in Mackay's prior work on equifinality, to identify critical parameters governing the observed temporal trends.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a validated modeling tool to quantify the 'environmental half-life' of banned POPs, linking regulatory action directly to measurable environmental outcomes. It will advance the application of fugacity models from steady-state assessments to dynamic forecasting, offering a synoptic indicator of contamination trends. The results will inform policy on the expected timelines for risk reduction and the relative importance of various environmental reservoirs.
proposals.novelty: While fugacity models are well-established, their integration of explicit kinetic constraints on SVOC partitioning—informed by recent findings on indoor dust—with time-variant emission scenarios to model phased-out chemicals is novel. This moves beyond equilibrium assumptions to mechanistically explain the observed hysteresis in environmental recovery post-ban.

proposals.title: Integrating Fugacity-Based Partitioning with High-Resolution Advection Schemes to Model the Global Transport and Bioaccumulation of Cyclic Volatile Methyl Siloxanes
proposals.motivation: Accurately predicting the long-range transport and bioaccumulation potential of persistent, bioaccumulative, and toxic (PBT) organic chemicals, such as cyclic volatile methyl siloxanes (cVMS), remains a major challenge in environmental chemistry. Current multi-media fate models often rely on simplified transport parameterizations, which fail to capture the complex, non-linear atmospheric and oceanic circulation patterns that govern global chemical redistribution and ultimate exposure concentrations.
proposals.hypothesis: Coupling a fugacity-based multi-media compartment model with high-resolution, mass-conserving advection schemes from climate models will significantly improve the spatiotemporal accuracy of predicted environmental concentrations and bioaccumulation metrics for semi-volatile organic chemicals (SVOCs) by better resolving their transport pathways and delays in phase equilibration.
proposals.method: We will adapt the transport algorithms (e.g., the semi-Lagrangian advection scheme from the Community Earth System Model) to track chemical mass in atmospheric and oceanic grids. This advection core will be dynamically coupled to a fugacity-based level III/IV model, where chemical activity governs partitioning between air, water, organic carbon, and aerosol phases. The model will be parameterized for cVMS (e.g., D4, D5, D6), incorporating pre-2026 data on degradation rates and partition coefficients. Simulations will be run at ~100 km resolution to assess global fate and compared to lower-resolution model outputs and monitoring data.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, physically robust framework for simulating the global fate of SVOCs, directly addressing uncertainties in environmental modeling highlighted in prior work. It will yield a synoptic indicator of contamination that integrates sophisticated transport with thermodynamic driving forces (fugacity/activity), offering improved risk assessment for chemicals with complex phase partitioning kinetics.
proposals.novelty: While fugacity models and climate advection schemes are mature independently, their integration to solve chemical transport problems is novel. This approach moves beyond simple inter-compartment transfer coefficients by using climate-model physics to simulate the advective movement of chemical mass, explicitly addressing kinetic delays in equilibration for low-volatility SVOCs during transport.

proposals.title: A Fugacity-Based Multi-Media Model to Quantify a Control Variable for the Novel Entities Planetary Boundary
proposals.motivation: The 'novel entities' planetary boundary, concerning anthropogenic chemical pollution, remains largely unquantified due to the immense diversity of substances and environmental compartments. Pre-2026 frameworks for assessing persistence, bioaccumulation, and toxicity (PBT) of individual chemicals lack the integrative, global-scale metrics needed to assess the boundary. A synoptic indicator, grounded in chemical thermodynamics and mass distribution, is urgently required to move from qualitative concern to quantitative assessment.
proposals.hypothesis: That a global-scale, multi-media fugacity model, using chemical activity as a unifying exposure metric, can integrate the diverse properties of thousands of organic chemicals to produce a quantifiable control variable (e.g., total anthropogenic chemical activity in the Earth system) for the novel entities boundary.
proposals.method: We will develop a Level IV fugacity model scaled to global environmental compartments (atmosphere, freshwater, ocean, soil, sediment). The model will be parameterized with pre-2026 chemical property databases (e.g., EPA's CompTox) for high-production-volume organic chemicals and SVOCs. It will incorporate kinetic delays in inter-compartmental partitioning, as identified in MacKay's prior work on indoor dust/air partitioning, to account for non-equilibrium conditions. Uncertainty and equifinality will be explicitly addressed using Monte Carlo analysis and sensitivity testing on key parameters (degradation rates, emission estimates).
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first mechanistic, mass-balance-based proposal for a quantifiable control variable for the novel entities boundary. It will deliver a scalable modeling framework that can integrate new chemical data and prioritize classes of concern based on their contribution to global chemical activity. The output will be a critical step towards operationalizing this planetary boundary for science and policy.
proposals.novelty: While fugacity models are established for single chemicals or regions, scaling this approach to a planetary boundary for the entirety of anthropogenic organic pollution is novel. The key innovation is using chemical activity, rather than mere concentration, as a synoptic, multi-media indicator of potential biological disruption and environmental contamination at a global scale.

proposals.title: Kinetic Delays and Reversibility in the Adsorption of Low-Volatility Organic Enhancers to Indoor Dust: An Experimental and Fugacity-Based Modeling Study
proposals.motivation: The environmental fate and human exposure risk of semi-volatile organic compounds (SVOCs), used as plasticizers, flame retardants, and other 'enhancers,' are critically governed by their partitioning to indoor dust. Pre-2026 models often assume instantaneous equilibrium, but evidence suggests kinetic delays, leading to significant uncertainty in predicting bioaccumulation and long-term toxicity. This gap limits the accuracy of fugacity-based multimedia models used for PBT (Persistence, Bioaccumulation, Toxicity) assessment of organic chemicals.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the adsorption of low-volatility organic enhancers to indoor dust is a kinetically limited process, with desorption exhibiting significant hysteresis, and that these non-equilibrium dynamics can be robustly parameterized within a fugacity modeling framework using a first-order kinetic representation.
proposals.method: We will experimentally characterize adsorption/desorption kinetics for a suite of model enhancers (e.g., phthalates, organophosphate esters) on standardized indoor dust in controlled chamber systems. Time-series measurements of chemical concentrations in air and dust will be performed using GC-MS. Data will be fitted to a first-order kinetic model to obtain partition rate constants. The derived parameters will be integrated into a dynamic, multi-compartment fugacity model, building on Dr. MacKay's established frameworks, to explicitly simulate kinetic delays and test model equifinality under varying environmental conditions.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first experimentally derived kinetic parameters for enhancer-dust partitioning, directly reducing a key uncertainty in indoor exposure models. It will yield a refined, functionally accurate representation of adsorption for multimedia fate models, improving predictions of bioaccumulation potential and long-term contamination synopses for SVOCs.
proposals.novelty: While kinetic delays have been noted for some SVOCs, a systematic experimental and modeling study targeting a broad class of 'enhancer' chemicals is lacking. This proposal uniquely integrates direct chamber experiments with the refinement of a fugacity-based synoptic indicator, moving beyond equilibrium assumptions to explicitly resolve time-dependent partitioning.

proposals.title: Dynamic Column Breakthrough Modeling of Co-Transport for Bioaccumulative Organic Chemicals in Saturated Porous Media: Integrating Fugacity, Activity, and Kinetic Delay
proposals.motivation: Predicting the environmental fate of persistent, bioaccumulative, and toxic (PBT) organic chemicals requires understanding their coupled transport and phase partitioning in heterogeneous porous media, such as soils and sediments. Pre-2026 models often assume local equilibrium, which fails for low-volatility, semi-volatile organic compounds (SVOCs) known to exhibit kinetic delays in partitioning, as shown in indoor dust/air systems. This gap limits accurate risk assessment of contaminant plumes and the design of remediation strategies.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that applying dynamic column breakthrough models from chemical engineering, which explicitly couple advection, dispersion, and non-equilibrium sorption/desorption, will more accurately simulate the co-transport of a target PBT chemical and a co-solvent or 'enhancer' by mechanistically representing kinetic delays and activity-dependent partitioning in a multi-phase porous system.
proposals.method: We will adapt and solve a dynamic, one-dimensional advection-dispersion-reaction model incorporating dual-species transport with competitive, kinetically limited sorption. The model will use a fugacity-based framework to calculate multi-phase partitioning (solid, water, air) and chemical activity as the driving force for interphase transfer. Kinetic rate constants for sorption/desorption will be derived from prior work on SVOCs. The system of partial differential equations will be solved numerically using a finite-difference method with operator splitting, calibrated and validated against existing column experiment data for siloxanes or similar SVOCs.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, mechanistic modeling tool that integrates key concepts from MacKay's prior work—fugacity, activity, and kinetic delay—into a dynamic transport context. It will advance environmental fate modeling by moving beyond equilibrium assumptions for SVOCs, offering a more robust framework for predicting bioaccumulation potential and long-range transport in groundwater and saturated zones.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the direct translation of chromatographic transport models from chemical engineering to simulate the co-transport of PBT chemicals with enhancers in environmental porous media, explicitly coupling non-equilibrium fugacity and activity principles within a dynamic, predictive breakthrough curve framework.

proposals.title: A QSAR-Integrated Fugacity Framework for High-Throughput Screening of Organic Contaminants: Application to PBT Assessment
proposals.motivation: Fugacity-based multimedia fate models are central to evaluating persistence, bioaccumulation, and toxicity (PBT) of organic chemicals, but their application is hindered by data gaps for key input parameters like partition coefficients and degradation rates. This limitation is acute for novel or poorly studied substances, impeding proactive risk assessment. Integrating Quantitative Structure-Activity Relationships (QSARs) directly into fugacity modeling could enable predictive screening from molecular structure alone, addressing this data scarcity.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a fugacity model parameterized entirely by QSAR-predicted physicochemical properties and reactivity estimates will yield screening-level environmental distributions and PBT indicators with acceptable uncertainty for a wide range of organic chemicals, providing a robust tool for prioritization.
proposals.method: We will develop a Level III fugacity model where all critical input parameters (air-water, octanol-water, and organic carbon-water partition coefficients; reaction rate constants in air, water, soil) are predicted using established, pre-2026 QSARs (e.g., EPI Suite, OPERA models, polyparameter linear free energy relationships). The model will incorporate uncertainty analysis via Monte Carlo simulation, propagating QSAR prediction errors and parameter covariance through to outputs like overall persistence and long-range transport potential. The framework will be validated against empirical monitoring data and traditional fugacity model results for a test set of well-studied SVOCs and emerging contaminants.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a fully operational, open-source modeling tool that links molecular structure directly to multimedia fate predictions, streamlining the early-tier assessment of data-poor chemicals. It will advance the field by formally quantifying how QSAR prediction uncertainty propagates into fugacity-based PBT indicators, a critical step for transparent application in regulatory screening.
proposals.novelty: While QSARs and fugacity models are mature tools, their integration into a unified, uncertainty-explicit framework for high-throughput, structure-based screening is novel. This moves beyond using QSARs for individual parameters to creating a synoptic, systems-level environmental indicator directly from a chemical's SMILES string.

proposals.title: A Fugacity-Based Framework for Modeling Competitive Sorption and Non-Ideal Interactions in Complex Organic Chemical Mixtures
proposals.motivation: Fugacity models are central to predicting the environmental fate and bioaccumulation potential of organic chemicals, but they typically treat chemicals in isolation. In real environments, chemicals exist as complex mixtures where competitive sorption to organic phases and non-ideal molecular interactions can significantly alter partitioning behavior and bioavailability. This limitation hinders accurate risk assessment of chemical cocktails, a critical pre-2026 regulatory challenge.
proposals.hypothesis: Incorporating competitive Langmuir or Ideal Adsorbed Solution Theory (IAST)-based sorption isotherms into a multi-media fugacity framework will significantly improve the prediction of phase distribution and activity for chemicals in mixtures, compared to models assuming independent partitioning.
proposals.method: We will extend a Level III/IV fugacity model to include competitive sorption modules for key phases (e.g., organic carbon, lipids, indoor dust). For solid phases, we will implement IAST using pre-2026 single-solute sorption parameters (e.g., Koc) to calculate mixture equilibria. For biotic phases, we will adapt a multi-chemical fugacity-based bioaccumulation model to account for competitive binding using chemical activity as the driving force. The model will be tested and calibrated using pre-existing mixture data for SVOCs (e.g., PAHs, PCBs, siloxanes) in environmental and indoor scenarios.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a practical, mechanistically grounded modeling tool to assess the PBT properties of chemicals in mixtures, moving beyond single-substance assessments. It will directly address the uncertainty and equifinality in environmental modeling highlighted in MacKay's prior work, offering a more synoptic indicator of contamination burden in complex systems.
proposals.novelty: While fugacity modeling and mixture interactions are established separately, their integration into a unified, activity-based framework for environmental fate prediction is novel. This approach explicitly links the kinetic delays in equilibration (as studied for SVOCs) with the thermodynamic constraints of multi-component systems.

proposals.title: Coupling Computational Fluid Dynamics with Fugacity-Based Models to Resolve Spatiotemporal Transport of SVOCs at the Indoor Environment Scale
proposals.motivation: Fugacity models are powerful for multi-media fate assessment but often rely on compartmental assumptions that average over critical sub-scale transport dynamics, particularly for low-volatility SVOCs where kinetic delays are significant. This limitation introduces uncertainty in predicting human exposure from indoor sources, as pre-2026 work by MacKay and others highlights the importance of non-equilibrium partitioning in dust/air systems. A high-resolution, physics-based approach is needed to mechanistically link source emissions to localized concentrations and bioaccumulation potential.
proposals.hypothesis: Integrating CFD-solved velocity and turbulence fields with a fugacity-driven transport model will significantly improve the spatiotemporal prediction of SVOC concentrations in indoor environments by explicitly resolving advective and diffusive processes that control kinetic equilibration delays between air, surfaces, and dust.
proposals.method: We will develop a coupled model framework using OpenFOAM, an open-source CFD solver. The Reynolds-Averaged Navier-Stokes (RANS) equations with a k-epsilon turbulence model will resolve room-scale airflow. A custom scalar transport solver will be implemented where chemical flux is driven by gradients in fugacity capacity (Z-values) and fugacity (f), rather than simple concentration. This fugacity-advection-diffusion equation will be solved for a target SVOC (e.g., a cyclic volatile methyl siloxane), with boundary conditions representing source/sink surfaces based on pre-2026 partition coefficient data and kinetic constraints for sorption.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a novel, high-resolution modeling tool that quantifies how airflow dynamics influence fugacity gradients and equilibrium timescales for SVOCs indoors. It will provide a more rigorous mechanistic basis for parameterizing multi-media box models and reduce equifinality by constraining transport rates with physical fluid dynamics, directly addressing uncertainty themes in MacKay's prior work.
proposals.novelty: While CFD is used for pollutant dispersion and fugacity models for regional fate, their explicit coupling to model chemical potential-driven transport of organics at the indoor micro-environment scale is novel. This moves beyond applying CFD solely to conserved tracers or using fugacity in well-mixed compartments.

proposals.title: Integrating Fugacity and Agent-Based Modeling to Simulate Bioaccumulation Dynamics of Organic Chemicals in Aquatic Food Webs
proposals.motivation: Predicting the bioaccumulation and biomagnification of organic chemicals in food webs remains a critical challenge in chemical risk assessment. Traditional fugacity-based multi-media models often rely on equilibrium assumptions and simplified trophic levels, which may not capture the kinetic delays, individual variability, and complex predator-prey interactions that govern real-world chemical transfer, as highlighted in recent work on SVOC partitioning and model equifinality.
proposals.hypothesis: An agent-based model (ABM) that simulates individual organisms with traits influencing chemical uptake and loss will reveal that variability in individual behavior and physiology, coupled with non-equilibrium trophic transfer, leads to emergent bioaccumulation patterns that deviate from predictions of conventional equilibrium-based fugacity models.
proposals.method: We will develop an ABM in NetLogo where agents represent individual organisms (e.g., plankton, small fish, predators) with species-specific traits (lipid content, feeding rate, growth). Chemical transfer will be governed by a bioaccumulation algorithm using organism-specific fugacity capacities (Z-values) and uptake/elimination rate constants. The model will simulate foraging, predation, and growth over time, tracking chemical mass in each agent. The fugacity-based bioaccumulation module will be rigorously benchmarked against established models (e.g., Gobas-type) before ABM integration.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, mechanistic framework for exploring how individual heterogeneity and ecological interactions influence bioaccumulation outcomes, moving beyond the steady-state, homogeneous assumptions of traditional models. It will yield a synoptic indicator of population- and community-level chemical body burdens that explicitly accounts for kinetic and ecological constraints.
proposals.novelty: While fugacity models are well-established for multi-media fate, their integration into an agent-based ecological framework to simulate bioaccumulation from a bottom-up, individual perspective is novel. This directly addresses the limitations of equilibrium assumptions and equifinality in current environmental modeling of organic pollutants.

proposals.title: A Fugacity-Based Model for the Partitioning and Time-Dependent Release of Hydrophobic Organic Contaminants from Microplastic Particles in Aquatic Systems
proposals.motivation: Microplastics are ubiquitous environmental contaminants and known vectors for organic pollutants, but quantitative models for chemical exchange are lacking. Pre-2026, most assessments treat microplastics as simple, equilibrium sorbents, ignoring the complex polymer-specific properties and kinetic limitations that control real-world fate and bioaccumulation potential. A mechanistic model is needed to predict the role of microplastics as a source or sink for toxic chemicals in water.
proposals.hypothesis: The partitioning and release of hydrophobic organic contaminants (HOCs) between microplastics and water can be accurately modeled using a fugacity-based framework that incorporates polymer-specific activity coefficients and explicitly addresses non-equilibrium kinetics due to intra-particle diffusion.
proposals.method: We will develop a Level III fugacity model, adapted for a microplastic-water system, where the plastic phase is characterized by its polymer-specific activity coefficient (γ_plastic), calculated using polyparameter linear free energy relationships (pp-LFERs) from pre-2026 literature. The model will treat the microplastic as a distinct compartment with a defined Z-value. Kinetic constraints on mass transfer will be incorporated using a radial diffusion algorithm within the particle, solving Fick's second law to simulate time-dependent release, parameterized with polymer-specific diffusion coefficients (D_plastic). The model will be validated against existing experimental data for HOC sorption/desorption kinetics on polyethylene, polypropylene, and polystyrene.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first fugacity-based modeling tool specifically designed to quantify chemical loadings and release rates from microplastics, moving beyond bulk sediment models. It will deliver a synoptic indicator of microplastic contamination potential and offer a mechanistic basis for improving PBT (Persistence, Bioaccumulation, Toxicity) assessments of HOCs in the presence of plastic debris.
proposals.novelty: While fugacity is well-established for bulk phases, its application to explicitly model the microplastic particle as a distinct compartment with intrinsic kinetic delays is novel. This integrates Mackay's expertise in fugacity, activity, and non-equilibrium partitioning (e.g., from dust/air studies) into the emerging microplastic vector research.

proposals.title: Beyond Structure: Quantifying the Role of Formulation Matrices and Environmental Conditions in Modulating the Permeability and Bioaccumulation Potential of Organic Chemicals
proposals.motivation: Current environmental fate and bioaccumulation models for organic chemicals, including those based on fugacity and activity, often rely on chemical structure-derived properties (e.g., log K_ow, molecular weight) to predict permeability across biological and environmental membranes. However, pre-2026 evidence, such as kinetic delays in dust/air partitioning for SVOCs, suggests that real-world matrices (e.g., indoor dust, plastic polymers, lipid vesicles) and formulation additives can significantly alter a chemical's effective activity and diffusive transport, leading to inaccurate risk assessments for PBT (Persistent, Bioaccumulative, Toxic) substances.
