proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that for low-volatility, semi-volatile, and ionizable organic compounds, the permeability governing bioaccumulation and environmental partitioning is not solely a function of molecular structure but is dominated by non-equilibrium interactions within complex formulation and environmental matrices, which modulate the chemical's effective fugacity and diffusivity.
proposals.method: We will employ a combined experimental and modeling approach. Experimentally, we will measure the time-dependent permeation of a suite of model organic compounds (e.g., cyclic volatile methyl siloxanes, phthalates, fluorotelomer alcohols) from standardized commercial formulations and from spiked indoor dust through synthetic polymeric and biological (e.g., PAMPA) membranes. The fugacity-based modeling framework, building on MacKay's previous work, will be extended by incorporating a dynamic, two-resistance model where the internal matrix resistance (formulation/dust) and the membrane resistance are explicitly parameterized and fitted to the kinetic permeation data using Bayesian calibration to address equifinality.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a quantitative, mechanistic framework to predict matrix-influenced permeability, moving beyond pure compound properties. It will directly improve bioaccumulation assessments by offering a more accurate descriptor of chemical activity in complex media, thereby refining PBT screening protocols. The results will also inform regulatory science on the importance of product formulation data for chemical safety evaluations.
proposals.novelty: While structure-activity relationships are well-established, this work explicitly tests the primacy of formulation effects over innate structure in controlling permeability, a paradigm shift. It uniquely integrates real-world matrix kinetics into a fugacity-activity modeling framework to resolve the uncertainty and equifinality identified in prior environmental models.

proposals.title: Inverse Property-Based Design: Defining Chemical Property Space for Target Permeability Rates in Environmental and Biological Barriers
proposals.motivation: Environmental fate and exposure models, including those for bioaccumulation, rely on predicting transport rates (e.g., membrane permeability, skin absorption, air-dust partitioning) from chemical properties. However, for designing safer chemicals or effective remediation agents, the inverse problem is critical: what combination of fundamental properties (e.g., log K_ow, molecular weight, H-bonding capacity) yields a desired, non-bioaccumulative transport rate? Current pre-2026 practice involves iterative forward modeling, which is inefficient and does not map the full feasible property space for a target outcome.
proposals.hypothesis: By inverting established quantitative structure-permeability relationships (QSPRs) and polyparameter linear free energy relationships (pp-LFERs) using constrained optimization and Bayesian inference, it is possible to define multi-dimensional chemical property envelopes that satisfy a specified permeability rate across key environmental compartments.
proposals.method: We will develop an inverse modeling framework using pre-2026 forward models for skin, biological membrane, and organic matter/air partitioning permeability as constraints. For well-parameterized pp-LFERs, we will employ numerical inversion (e.g., using pre-2026 SciPy optimizers) to solve for descriptor vectors. For more complex QSPRs, we will implement a Markov Chain Monte Carlo (MCMC) sampling approach within defined, physically plausible property bounds to generate posterior distributions of property combinations that meet the target rate. The approach will explicitly incorporate parameter uncertainty from the underlying forward models, a focus of MacKay's prior work.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel methodology for the 'benign-by-design' and green chemistry communities, translating a target environmental performance metric (low permeability/bioaccumulation potential) into a tangible chemical design space. It will also reveal the equifinality in property combinations leading to similar transport rates, informing risk assessment of chemical classes beyond traditional single-property triggers.
proposals.novelty: While forward prediction of permeability is standard, systematically inverting these models to delineate the complete property space for a target rate is a non-trivial, under-explored computational challenge. This moves beyond sensitivity analysis to a generative design and feasibility assessment tool grounded in mechanistic environmental chemistry.

proposals.title: A Constraint-Based Network Model for Organic Aerosol Aging: Predicting Chemical Pathways and Impacts on Pollutant Partitioning
proposals.motivation: The dynamic chemical aging of organic aerosol (OA) is a critical, yet poorly constrained, process in atmospheric chemistry and climate models. This aging alters OA's physical properties and chemical matrix, which in turn governs the gas-particle partitioning and long-range fate of co-occurring persistent organic pollutants (POPs). Current models lack a mechanistically rich, yet computationally tractable, framework to predict the dominant transformation pathways of complex organic carbon and their consequences for POP bioaccumulation potential.
proposals.hypothesis: By adapting constraint-based modeling from metabolic pathway analysis, we hypothesize that the complex, non-linear aging of atmospheric organic carbon can be represented as a network of plausible chemical transformations, whose dominant fluxes under environmental constraints can predict the evolving OA composition and its capacity to sequester semi-volatile organic pollutants.
proposals.method: We will construct a stoichiometric matrix (S) where rows represent chemical species (primary/secondary organic compounds, oxidation states) and columns represent known/hypothesized reactions (e.g., OH oxidation, oligomerization). Using Flux Balance Analysis (FBA), a linear programming algorithm, we will solve for the reaction flux vector (v) that maximizes an objective function (e.g., production of low-volatility oxygenated OA) subject to constraints derived from pre-2026 chamber/field data (oxidant fluxes, radical budgets). The model will be iteratively coupled to a partitioning module to simulate dynamic POP sorption.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, systems-level framework to simulate OA chemical evolution, moving beyond empirical parameterizations. It will directly link OA aging chemistry to predictions of POP partitioning coefficients (K_OA), offering a dynamic, mechanism-based tool to improve assessments of chemical persistence, long-range transport, and bioaccumulation in multimedia fate models.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the direct conceptual and methodological analogy from systems biology (FBA) to atmospheric aerosol chemistry, creating a constraint-based network model for abiotic chemical aging—a significant departure from traditional kinetic or equilibrium-based atmospheric models.

proposals.title: A Closed-Loop Framework for Dynamic Reaction Network Mapping and Data Assimilation to Predict the Fate of Bioaccumulative Organic Contaminants
proposals.motivation: Predicting the environmental fate of organic chemicals is limited by the intractability of modeling their evolving properties, as degradation pathways and transformation products are often unknown or poorly quantified. This uncertainty, a key focus of MacKay's prior work on fugacity, activity, and equifinality, undermines risk assessments for persistent, bioaccumulative, and toxic (PBT) substances. A paradigm shift from static to dynamic, evidence-based fate modeling is urgently needed.
proposals.hypothesis: That integrating high-resolution mass spectrometry (HRMS)-based non-target analysis with a Bayesian data assimilation framework can empirically constrain dynamic chemical reaction networks, significantly reducing equifinality and improving the real-time predictive accuracy of contaminant fate models for key SVOCs.
proposals.method: We will first employ LC/GC-HRMS for non-target analysis of water and indoor dust samples to empirically identify major transformation products and infer degradation pathways for model cyclic volatile methyl siloxanes (cVMS) and other SVOCs. These pathways will be formalized into a dynamic reaction network within a fugacity-based multimedia model. A sequential Monte Carlo (particle filter) data assimilation algorithm will then be implemented to continuously update the model's reaction rates and species concentrations by assimilating near-real-time sensor data (e.g., passive samplers, GC-MS outputs) from a controlled environmental test system.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a novel, operational closed-loop modeling system that moves beyond the assumption of chemical stability. It will deliver a publicly accessible library of prioritized transformation pathways for high-concern SVOCs and demonstrate a method to reduce predictive uncertainty in fate models through continuous data integration, directly addressing challenges highlighted in the PBT perspective and equifinality papers.
proposals.novelty: The approach uniquely combines non-target analytical chemistry for pathway discovery with Bayesian data assimilation in a reactive transport model, creating a self-correcting forecasting tool. This moves beyond prior equilibrium-focused partitioning studies (like the indoor dust kinetic delay work) to dynamically resolve kinetic degradation processes in complex matrices.

proposals.title: Constraint-Based, Dynamic Fate Modeling of Organic Contaminants: A Flux Balance Analysis Approach for Transient Subsurface Networks
proposals.motivation: Current multimedia fate models for persistent, bioaccumulative, and toxic (PBT) organic chemicals often rely on static partition coefficients and first-order degradation constants, treating the environment as a steady-state system. This fails to capture the dynamic, interdependent biogeochemical reactions in the subsurface that control the transient formation and transport of toxic transformation products, leading to significant uncertainty in long-term risk predictions. A new modeling paradigm is needed to systematically account for these time-varying constraints on contaminant fate.
proposals.hypothesis: By adapting the principles of dynamic Flux Balance Analysis (dFBA) from systems biology, a constraint-based reaction network model can more accurately predict the time-evolving speciation and transport of organic contaminants and their degradation products in response to fluctuating environmental conditions than conventional fugacity- or activity-based steady-state models.
proposals.method: We will construct a stoichiometric reaction network where contaminants and their known biotic/abiotic transformation products are metabolites, and degradation pathways are reactions constrained by time-series data on electron acceptor availability, microbial biomass, and pH. The core algorithm will be a linear programming solver (e.g., COBRA toolbox) maximizing a pseudo-objective (e.g., total degradation flux) subject to these dynamic constraints, integrated with a 1-D advection-dispersion transport module. The model will be parameterized and tested for a case-study system (e.g., chlorinated ethenes or cyclic volatile methyl siloxanes) using literature data on kinetic delays in phase partitioning and degradation pathways.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel computational framework that explicitly links transient environmental conditions to contaminant transformation and transport, reducing equifinality in model calibrations. It will yield a synoptic indicator of contamination that dynamically accounts for speciation, moving beyond the parent compound to predict the fate of potentially more toxic or mobile intermediates.
proposals.novelty: This represents the first direct application of constraint-based metabolic network analysis, a cornerstone of systems biology, to environmental fate modeling of organic pollutants. It fundamentally shifts from assuming constant chemical properties to simulating them as emergent outcomes of a dynamically constrained biogeochemical network.

proposals.title: Dynamic Fugacity Modeling of Organic Contaminants: Integrating Transformation Pathways and Time-Varying Properties for Improved Bioaccumulation and Transport Predictions
proposals.motivation: Fugacity-based environmental fate models, central to PBT (Persistence, Bioaccumulation, Toxicity) assessment, typically assume parent chemicals have stable properties. However, real-world conditions (e.g., photolysis, hydrolysis, biodegradation) generate transformation products with distinct partitioning behavior, while factors like temperature and organic carbon content fluctuate. This static assumption introduces significant uncertainty, as evidenced in prior work on cyclic volatile methyl siloxanes, and may misrepresent long-term bioaccumulation and multimedia transport.
proposals.hypothesis: Explicitly modeling the formation and fate of key transformation products within a dynamic fugacity framework, where chemical properties and environmental parameters vary over time, will significantly reduce uncertainty and improve the accuracy of long-range transport and bioaccumulation predictions for organic contaminants compared to conventional steady-state models.
proposals.method: We will develop a dynamic Level IV fugacity model, extending from Dr. MacKay's prior work. The model will incorporate kinetic degradation modules for parent compounds (e.g., SVOCs, pesticides) to generate major transformation products. Time-series data for temperature and particulate organic matter will drive seasonally variable partition coefficients (K_OW, K_OA) and degradation rates. The model will be implemented using the R programming language, employing the 'deSolve' package for numerical integration of differential equations describing mass balance across compartments (air, water, soil, sediment, biota). Case studies will include chemicals known for significant transformation (e.g., certain fluorotelomer alcohols) and will be calibrated/validated using historical monitoring data.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, open-source modeling framework that quantifies the impact of chemical transformation and environmental dynamics on fate predictions. It will deliver a more robust 'synoptic indicator' of contamination that accounts for evolving chemical mixtures. The results will directly inform regulatory risk assessment by highlighting when static models fail and by offering a path to reduce equifinality in model calibration.
proposals.novelty: While transformation products are sometimes modeled statically, their integrated, kinetic generation and fate within a dynamic fugacity model—coupled with time-varying physicochemical parameters—represents a significant advance beyond standard steady-state assumptions. This directly addresses the 'kinetic delay' and equifinality challenges identified in Dr. MacKay's recent research.

proposals.title: A Fugacity-Based Framework for Predicting the Bioaccumulation Potential and Mixture Toxicity of Complex Organic Contaminant Mixtures in Aquatic Systems
proposals.motivation: Environmental risk assessment remains largely chemical-by-chemical, despite organisms being exposed to complex, dynamic mixtures of organic contaminants. This single-substance focus fails to account for mixture effects, such as concentration addition or interactions affecting bioavailability and bioaccumulation, leading to potential underestimation of risk. A multi-media, mechanistic framework is needed to prioritize mixtures of concern, especially for persistent, bioaccumulative, and toxic (PBT) chemicals in water and sediment.
proposals.hypothesis: The overall bioaccumulation potential and toxic activity of a complex organic contaminant mixture in an aquatic organism can be predicted by the summed fugacities (or activities) of its components, adjusted for kinetic limitations in partitioning and biomagnification, providing a more accurate synoptic indicator of risk than individual chemical concentrations.
proposals.method: We will develop a dynamic, multi-compartment fugacity model extended to handle mixtures. The model will integrate: 1) chemical-specific input parameters (Kow, Kaw, Koa) for a priority set of SVOCs and CVMSs, 2) kinetic rate constants for air/dust/water/organism partitioning informed by Mackay's prior work, and 3) a probabilistic module to propagate parameter uncertainty and assess equifinality. The core algorithm will sum component fugacities in organism compartments to estimate a 'Mixture Fugacity Burden.' This will be correlated with in vitro bioassay data (e.g., aryl hydrocarbon receptor activity) from field-collected water and biota samples to test the toxicity linkage.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, mechanistically grounded modeling tool to screen and rank environmental mixtures based on their combined bioaccumulation and toxic potential. It will advance the field by moving beyond concentration-based mixture assessment to an activity/fugacity basis, directly linking multi-media fate modeling with mixture toxicology. The framework will offer regulators a practical synoptic indicator for complex contamination.
proposals.novelty: While fugacity models exist for single chemicals, applying the fugacity/activity concept to predict the integrated burden and effect of whole mixtures is novel. The approach uniquely combines Mackay's expertise in fugacity modeling, kinetic partitioning delays, and uncertainty analysis to solve the mixture prioritization problem.

proposals.title: Data-Assimilative Reactive Transport Modeling: A 4D-Var Framework for Reducing Uncertainty in Forecasts of Organic Contaminant Plume Evolution
proposals.motivation: Predicting the fate of organic contaminants in groundwater is critical for risk assessment and remediation, but forecasts are highly uncertain due to poorly constrained kinetic and equilibrium parameters. Current models are often calibrated statically, failing to leverage the growing volume of high-frequency, multi-parameter monitoring data. This limits the translation of foundational chemical fate knowledge into reliable, site-specific predictions.
proposals.hypothesis: A data assimilation framework, adapted from atmospheric chemistry, can dynamically integrate real-time monitoring data with a process-based reactive transport model to significantly reduce uncertainty in key biogeochemical parameters (e.g., degradation rates, sorption coefficients) and improve the accuracy of plume evolution forecasts.
proposals.method: We will develop a hybrid system coupling a modular reactive transport model (e.g., PFLOTRAN or PHREEQC) with a four-dimensional variational (4D-Var) data assimilation engine. The model will encode physical transport and key fate processes (sorption, biodegradation, hydrolysis) for representative organic contaminants. The 4D-Var algorithm will iteratively adjust uncertain parameters by minimizing the misfit between model predictions and time-series field data (e.g., contaminant concentrations, redox indicators, geochemical tracers) over a defined assimilation window.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a novel, operational framework for dynamic groundwater model calibration, directly reducing predictive uncertainty for contaminant transport. It will demonstrate how continuous data integration transforms static process knowledge into adaptive forecasts, offering a powerful tool for managing sites contaminated with persistent, bioaccumulative, and toxic (PBT) organic chemicals.
proposals.novelty: While data assimilation is mature in atmospheric sciences, its application to groundwater reactive transport for organic chemicals is nascent. This proposal uniquely adapts the sophisticated 4D-Var approach to simultaneously constrain both physical and complex biogeochemical uncertainties, moving beyond traditional hydraulic calibration.

proposals.title: Integrating Fugacity-Based Activity and Kinetic Sorption Constraints into Groundwater Transport Models for Bioaccumulative Organic Contaminants
proposals.motivation: Predictive models for groundwater transport of persistent, bioaccumulative, and toxic (PBT) organic chemicals often rely on equilibrium partitioning assumptions, which can fail for low-volatility, hydrophobic compounds. This is critical because such models underpin risk assessments for drinking water sources and ecosystem health. Recent work, including MacKay's, highlights significant kinetic delays in environmental media partitioning and equifinality in model outputs, suggesting current groundwater fate predictions may be highly uncertain.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a groundwater transport model incorporating (1) a fugacity/activity framework to describe multi-phase partitioning and (2) explicit kinetic rate laws for solid-phase sorption/desorption will significantly reduce predictive uncertainty and more accurately simulate the long-term fate and bioaccumulation potential of SVOCs compared to equilibrium-based models.
proposals.method: We will develop a one-dimensional reactive transport model in Python, building on the fugacity approach. The model will treat sorption/desorption for low-volatility SVOCs using a first-order kinetic algorithm, parameterized with data from dust/air partitioning studies. We will apply the model to cyclic volatile methyl siloxanes (cVMS) and legacy chlorinated organics, calibrating it against existing column experiment data and using Monte Carlo simulation to quantify uncertainty and equifinality in parameter sets, explicitly comparing results to an equilibrium sub-model.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a mechanistically improved modeling framework that explicitly links groundwater transport processes to bioaccumulation endpoints via the chemical activity metric. It will deliver a quantitative assessment of how kinetic constraints alter long-term contamination plumes and bioavailability, directly informing PBT assessment under pre-2026 regulatory paradigms. The open-source model will serve as a tool for testing the environmental fate of emerging organic contaminants.
proposals.novelty: While fugacity models are established, their integration with non-equilibrium sorption kinetics specifically for groundwater transport of SVOCs is novel. This directly addresses the 'kinetic delay' phenomenon identified in indoor environments but largely unincorporated into subsurface fate models, moving beyond the limitations of equilibrium Kd coefficients.

proposals.title: A Fugacity-Based Multi-Compartment Model for Predicting the Long-Term Fate and Bioaccumulation Potential of Organic Contaminants in Heterogeneous Aquifers
proposals.motivation: Predicting the long-term fate of persistent organic chemicals in aquifers remains a critical challenge for risk assessment. Traditional advection-dispersion models often fail to capture the complex sequestration and slow release from low-permeability zones and variably-sorbing sediments, leading to significant uncertainty in plume persistence and bioaccumulation potential. A more integrated, mass-balance framework is needed to account for these heterogeneous processes.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the fate of organic contaminants in heterogeneous aquifers can be accurately modeled by adapting fugacity-based, multi-compartment ecosystem models, where chemical activity (fugacity) drives inter-compartmental transfer, and that this approach will reveal dominant sequestration pathways controlling long-term plume behavior and bioaccumulation.
proposals.method: We will define aquifer compartments (e.g., mobile groundwater, immobile water in low-permeability zones, and sediment organic carbon fractions) and parameterize them using site data. Chemical transfer fluxes (advection, diffusion, sorption/desorption, biodegradation) between compartments will be formulated using fugacity and first-order rate constants. The system of mass-balance differential equations will be solved numerically, with sensitivity and uncertainty analysis (e.g., Monte Carlo) applied to key parameters like organic carbon-water partition coefficients and mass transfer rates, following principles from Mackay's previous work on uncertainty and equifinality.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, integrated modeling tool that quantifies the role of aquifer heterogeneity in contaminant sequestration and persistence. It will deliver a synoptic indicator of contamination state across phases and directly link subsurface fate to bioaccumulation potential in groundwater ecosystems, advancing beyond traditional transport models.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the explicit adaptation of fugacity-based ecosystem compartment models, commonly used for surface environments, to structured aquifer systems, and in directly coupling physical aquifer heterogeneity with chemical activity to predict bioaccumulation.

proposals.title: A Fugacity-Based, Spatially Explicit Regional Model for Assessing the Fate and Bioaccumulation Potential of Organic Contaminants Across Hydrological and Land-Use Gradients
proposals.motivation: Current contaminant fate models are often limited to specific media or local scales, failing to capture the integrated, regional-scale transport and partitioning of chemicals across complex landscapes. This gap hinders accurate risk assessment for bioaccumulative and toxic (PBT) organic chemicals, as regional hydrology and heterogeneous land use (e.g., urban, agricultural) critically control chemical loading, transport, and long-term exposure. A scalable framework is needed to synthesize these drivers for proactive chemical management.
proposals.hypothesis: Integrating a fugacity-based chemical fate and bioaccumulation model with a spatially explicit hydrological and land-use framework will significantly improve the prediction of regional-scale chemical concentrations and identify landscape 'hotspots' of elevated exposure risk that are missed by simpler, homogeneous models.
proposals.method: We will develop a coupled model architecture. The core is a Level III/IV fugacity model, parameterized for key organic chemical classes (e.g., SVOCs, cyclic siloxanes), to handle multi-media partitioning and non-equilibrium kinetics, as informed by MacKay's prior work. This will be embedded within a GIS-based spatial grid, where each cell's fugacity capacity and inter-compartment transport fluxes are dynamically determined by local parameters (soil type, land cover, water body type) sourced from regional databases. Hydrological routing algorithms (e.g., based on digital elevation models) will govern advective transport between cells. The model will be implemented in Python, using sensitivity and uncertainty analysis frameworks to address equifinality.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, open-source tool for regional-scale chemical fate and exposure assessment, directly linking landscape properties to PBT outcomes. It will advance the field by demonstrating how fugacity and activity concepts can be operationalized at large scales, offering a synoptic indicator of contamination that supports regulatory prioritization and targeted monitoring.
proposals.novelty: While fugacity models and spatial hydrology models exist independently, their tight integration into a single predictive framework for organic contaminants at a regional scale is novel. The explicit incorporation of kinetic limitations in partitioning (e.g., for dust/air or sediment/water) within a dynamic spatial model addresses a key simplification in existing large-scale assessments.

proposals.title: Inverse Source Apportionment of High-Priority Organic Contaminants Using Fugacity-Based Bayesian Inference and Monitoring Network Data
proposals.motivation: Regulators urgently need to identify emission sources of high-priority chemicals like PFAS and novel flame retardants to enable targeted control, but traditional forward modeling is hampered by uncertain emission inventories. Simultaneously, exposure monitoring networks (e.g., in water, air, or via wastewater-based epidemiology) generate concentration data that implicitly encodes source information, yet this data is underutilized for quantitative source attribution. Bridging this gap is critical for moving from exposure observation to effective intervention.
proposals.hypothesis: The unique environmental fate and transport signatures (e.g., partitioning coefficients, degradation rates) of high-priority organic chemicals, formalized within a fugacity-based multimedia model, can be leveraged within a Bayesian inversion framework to probabilistically apportion measured environmental concentrations to specific geographic source regions and emission timelines.
proposals.method: We will develop a source inversion algorithm coupling a fugacity-based multimedia fate and transport model (extending Mackay's well-established framework) with a Markov Chain Monte Carlo (MCMC) sampler. The forward model will incorporate chemical-specific properties (e.g., KOW, KOA, half-lives) and landscape parameters. The inverse algorithm will assimilate time-series concentration data from selected monitoring networks to solve for the most probable spatial emission fields and their temporal dynamics, explicitly quantifying uncertainty through the posterior distributions. The approach will be tested and refined using synthetic data and applied to a case study of perfluorooctanoic acid (PFOA) in a watershed with known and suspected point sources.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, chemistry-informed methodology for quantitatively linking population exposure data to probable emission sources, directly addressing a key regulatory need. It will advance environmental modeling by formally integrating Mackay's fugacity/activity concepts with inverse techniques, creating a transparent framework for handling equifinality and uncertainty in source attribution for organic pollutants.
proposals.novelty: While inverse modeling is used in hydrology and air quality, its tailored application to high-priority organic chemicals using their specific partitioning and bioaccumulation potential within a fugacity-based Bayesian framework is novel. This moves beyond conventional forward PBT assessment to active source inference, directly building on Mackay's recent work on fugacity synoptic indicators and kinetic delays in partitioning.

proposals.title: Molecular Dynamics and Machine Learning for Predicting Partitioning Coefficients of Persistent Organic Pollutants
proposals.motivation: Predicting the environmental fate of Persistent Organic Pollutants (POPs) relies on empirical partition coefficients (e.g., K_ow, K_oa) derived from bulk measurements, which fail to capture molecular-scale interactions and kinetic delays in complex media like indoor dust. This gap introduces significant uncertainty in multimedia fugacity models used for PBT (Persistence, Bioaccumulation, Toxicity) assessment and regulatory decision-making. A mechanistic, molecular-scale understanding is needed to improve predictions of long-range transport and bioaccumulation potential.
proposals.hypothesis: That the equilibrium partitioning behavior of POPs across environmental phases can be accurately predicted from first-principles molecular simulations, and that machine learning models trained on these molecular descriptors can outperform traditional quantitative structure-activity relationship (QSAR) models based on bulk properties.
proposals.method: We will use all-atom molecular dynamics (MD) simulations with OPLS-AA/GAFF force fields in GROMACS to compute free energy profiles (via thermodynamic integration or umbrella sampling) for model POPs (e.g., PCBs, siloxanes) transferring between water, organic phases, and model dust surfaces. Key molecular descriptors (e.g., interaction energies, solvation shells, diffusion coefficients) will be extracted. These descriptors will train a graph neural network (GNN) architecture (e.g., MPNN) to predict partition coefficients for a broader chemical space, validated against existing experimental data.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, mechanistic link between molecular-scale interactions and macro-scale environmental partitioning, directly addressing uncertainties in fugacity-based models highlighted in MacKay's prior work. It will deliver a validated computational tool for predicting partition coefficients for novel or data-poor chemicals, enhancing the assessment of their PBT properties and long-range transport potential.
proposals.novelty: While MD is used in drug discovery, its application to predict environmental partition coefficients for POPs, integrated with GNNs to bridge scales, is novel. This moves beyond correlative QSARs and bulk fugacity concepts to a physics-based, predictive framework for environmental fate.

proposals.title: Closing the Loop: An Active Learning Framework for Uncertainty-Quantified, Multi-Tiered Hazard Assessment of Organic Chemicals
proposals.motivation: High-throughput screening (HTS) models for chemical hazard are essential for managing vast chemical inventories but suffer from well-documented accuracy-reliability trade-offs, especially for novel or sparsely tested compounds. Regulatory decisions based on such predictions require a transparent understanding of their uncertainty and the evidence underpinning them, a challenge highlighted in recent work on equifinality in environmental models. Current tiered assessment approaches lack a systematic, automated feedback mechanism to iteratively improve predictive models with targeted high-fidelity data.
proposals.hypothesis: An active learning framework that strategically directs higher-tier experimental or simulation resources based on Tier-1 prediction uncertainty and chemical similarity to known toxicants will produce more reliable hazard assessments and continuously improve the underlying QSAR models through a closed-loop feedback system.
proposals.method: The framework will integrate: 1) Tier-1 models (e.g., random forest or graph neural network QSARs) that output hazard predictions with uncertainty estimates via conformal prediction or ensemble variance. 2) A decision engine that prioritizes chemicals for Tier-2 analysis (e.g., in vitro bioassays or fugacity-based multimedia fate simulations) based on high uncertainty, structural alerts, and estimated exposure potential from pre-2026 bioaccumulation models. 3) An automated feedback loop where Tier-2 results retrain the Tier-1 models, implementing a Bayesian optimization strategy to refine the acquisition function for selecting subsequent Tier-2 candidates.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a practical, self-improving computational system that provides regulators with hazard assessments accompanied by quantifiable confidence scores and a clear audit trail of evidence. It directly addresses the equifinality and uncertainty challenges in organic pollutant modeling by embedding iterative validation into the assessment workflow, moving beyond static tiered approaches.
proposals.novelty: The integration of active learning directly into a regulatory-tiered assessment framework to create a closed-loop, self-validating system is novel. It explicitly uses uncertainty quantification not just as an output metric, but as the primary driver for targeted data acquisition and model refinement, building upon but significantly extending pre-2026 concepts of fugacity-based indicators and kinetic partitioning studies.

proposals.title: A Tiered, Multi-Model Ensemble Framework for Quantifying Exposure and Uncertainty of Bioaccumulative Organic Chemicals
proposals.motivation: Regulatory prioritization of persistent, bioaccumulative, and toxic (PBT) organic chemicals is hampered by high uncertainty in exposure quantification, stemming from variable model structures and parameterizations. Existing approaches, from high-throughput screening to complex fugacity-based multimedia models, are applied in isolation, lacking a formal mechanism to synthesize their predictions and characterize consensus. This gap leads to inconsistent risk rankings and impedes efficient chemical management.
proposals.hypothesis: A coordinated, tiered model intercomparison project, analogous to climate model ensembles, will produce more robust and consensus-driven quantitative exposure estimates for priority organic chemicals, with significantly better-characterized uncertainty than any single-model approach.
proposals.method: We will establish a three-tiered framework: Tier 1 uses high-throughput quantitative structure-activity relationship (QSAR) models for initial screening. Tier 2 applies steady-state multimedia fate models (e.g., EQC, BETR) for refined assessment. Tier 3 employs dynamic, probabilistic models incorporating kinetic delays (e.g., for indoor dust/air partitioning) and bioaccumulation. A formal model intercomparison exercise will be conducted for a curated set of high-priority chemicals (e.g., cyclic volatile methyl siloxanes), where participating models from each tier will run standardized scenarios. Outputs will be analyzed using ensemble statistics and variance decomposition to quantify inter-model variability and identify key sources of uncertainty.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a standardized, transparent protocol for generating consensus exposure estimates with quantified uncertainty, directly supporting regulatory decisions under programs like REACH and the Stockholm Convention. It will also create a benchmark dataset and identify the most critical processes (e.g., kinetic limitations, degradation rates) driving model divergence for different chemical classes.
proposals.novelty: While model intercomparisons exist, applying a coordinated, tiered ensemble approach—inspired by climate science—to organic contaminant exposure assessment is novel. It systematically bridges high-throughput prioritization and detailed mechanistic modeling within a unified uncertainty quantification framework.

proposals.title: A Graph Neural Network Framework for Predicting Environmental Fate Parameters of Organic Chemicals from Molecular Structure
proposals.motivation: Predicting the environmental fate and exposure potential of organic chemicals is critical for risk assessment but remains challenging due to the complexity of multi-media partitioning and degradation. Current methods, such as property estimation based on fugacity and activity, often rely on discrete, experimentally derived parameters and struggle with uncertainty and kinetic delays in equilibration. A unified, structure-based predictive tool could transform screening by directly linking molecular features to key fate processes.
proposals.hypothesis: Graph neural network architectures, proven successful in predicting drug pharmacokinetics (ADME) from molecular structure, can be adapted to learn the complex, non-linear relationships between the graph structure of organic chemicals and their critical environmental fate parameters, providing accurate, generalizable predictions.
proposals.method: We will adapt a state-of-the-art GNN architecture, such as a Message Passing Neural Network (MPNN) or Attentive FP, used in drug-target interaction modeling. The model will be trained on curated environmental chemistry datasets (e.g., EPI Suite, NORMAN) linking SMILES representations to target parameters like log Koc, biodegradation half-lives, and air-water partition coefficients. Training will involve a multi-task learning setup to predict multiple fate properties simultaneously, leveraging shared molecular representations. Model performance will be rigorously validated against held-out experimental data and compared to traditional QSAR methods.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, open-source computational tool for rapid, structure-based screening of chemical fate, reducing reliance on discrete experimental inputs. It will advance the field by offering a synoptic, data-driven indicator of environmental contamination potential that integrates multiple fate processes, directly addressing challenges of uncertainty and equifinality in environmental modeling highlighted in prior work.
proposals.novelty: While GNNs are established in drug discovery, their application to predicting environmental fate parameters by directly analogizing the body's ADME processes to environmental compartments is novel. This approach moves beyond traditional single-parameter QSARs to a unified model capturing the complex interplay of substructures with multi-media partitioning and degradation.

proposals.title: Bayesian Source Inversion with Structure-Informed Priors: A Machine Learning Framework for Attributing Organic Contaminants
proposals.motivation: Identifying sources and forecasting the fate of organic contaminants is critical for risk assessment and remediation, but remains challenging due to sparse monitoring data and uncertainty in chemical-specific transport parameters. Traditional inverse models often rely on generic or poorly constrained priors, especially for novel or data-poor substances, limiting their predictive power and reliability in multi-media environments.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that integrating machine learning models, which predict key fate parameters (e.g., partition coefficients, degradation rates) from molecular structure, into a Bayesian inversion framework will significantly improve the accuracy and domain of applicability for contaminant source attribution and fate prediction.
proposals.method: We will develop a two-stage framework. First, we will train graph neural networks (GNNs) and molecular fingerprint-based models on pre-2026 databases (e.g., EPI Suite, NORMAN) to predict chemical-specific fugacity capacities, reaction rates, and bioaccumulation factors. Second, these ML-predicted parameters will define the prior distributions and likelihood within a Markov Chain Monte Carlo (MCMC) inversion solver, which assimilates observed concentration data from water, air, and dust to infer posterior distributions for source location, magnitude, and release history.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, chemically intelligent tool for environmental forensics that can attribute contamination sources for both well-studied and poorly characterized organic chemicals. It will advance the field by formally integrating predictive cheminformatics into environmental fate modeling, reducing equifinality and offering a systematic approach to quantify uncertainty in source apportionment.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the direct fusion of ML-based chemical property prediction with Bayesian inversion to create chemical-specific priors, moving beyond generic 'chemical space' representations to a fully integrated, predictive attribution system for novel substances.

proposals.title: Predicting Multi-Media Fugacity and Bioaccumulation Potential of Organic Chemicals Using Graph Neural Networks and Molecular Descriptors
proposals.motivation: Predicting the environmental fate and exposure of organic chemicals remains a complex challenge due to the interplay of partitioning, degradation, and bioaccumulation across multiple media. Traditional fugacity-based models and PBT assessments rely on measured or estimated physicochemical properties, which are often uncertain or unavailable for new compounds. A robust, data-driven method to predict key fate parameters directly from molecular structure would accelerate chemical safety screening and address the equifinality and kinetic limitations highlighted in recent work.
proposals.hypothesis: Machine learning models trained on high-quality experimental datasets can accurately predict critical environmental fate parameters (e.g., partition coefficients, degradation half-lives) and bioaccumulation metrics from chemical structure alone, outperforming traditional group contribution methods and reducing uncertainty in multimedia exposure models.
proposals.method: We will compile a curated database of experimental data for key fate parameters (Kow, Kaw, Koa, degradation rates) and bioaccumulation factors (BAF, BCF) from pre-2026 sources. Molecular structures will be encoded using both traditional descriptors (e.g., from RDKit) and learned representations via Graph Neural Networks (GNNs), specifically Message Passing Neural Networks (MPNNs). We will train and compare ensemble models (Random Forest, Gradient Boosting) and deep learning models (MPNNs) to predict the target parameters, using the models to then estimate steady-state fugacities and a synoptic contamination indicator for a broad chemical space.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, openly accessible QSAR framework for multimedia fate and exposure prediction, directly linking chemical structure to fugacity-based indicators. It will advance the field by integrating mechanistic fate modeling with modern machine learning, offering a more efficient path for screening-level PBT assessment and identifying chemicals where kinetic delays in partitioning (e.g., for SVOCs in dust) may be most significant.
proposals.novelty: While QSARs exist for individual properties, the direct prediction of a multi-media fugacity-based contamination indicator from structure using GNNs is novel. This approach moves beyond single-parameter estimation to provide a holistic, systems-level exposure prediction that inherently accounts for the interconnected fate processes central to the researcher's previous work on fugacity and equifinality.

proposals.title: Bayesian Source Apportionment of Organic Chemical Exposures: A Receptor Modeling Framework for Human Biomonitoring and Environmental Data
proposals.motivation: Human exposure to complex mixtures of organic chemicals is a major public health concern, but linking measured concentrations in biomonitoring or environmental media to their original sources remains a significant challenge. Current methods often lack a formal, quantitative framework to deconvolute the contributions of multiple, co-occurring sources like consumer products, diet, and indoor environments. This gap hinders effective exposure mitigation and risk management.
proposals.hypothesis: A Bayesian receptor modeling framework, adapted from atmospheric source apportionment, can successfully quantify the proportional contributions of distinct source categories (e.g., specific plastics, pesticides, dietary components) to observed chemical concentrations in human biospecimens and paired environmental samples.
proposals.method: We will adapt the Positive Matrix Factorization (PMF) and Chemical Mass Balance (CMB) receptor model concepts into a unified Bayesian hierarchical model. The model will use chemical 'fingerprints' (diagnostic ratios of organic compounds and their metabolites) from candidate sources as informative priors. Observational data will include paired measurements of SVOCs and their transformation products in indoor dust, air, and human serum or urine from existing cohorts. Markov Chain Monte Carlo (MCMC) sampling will be used for inference, solving the inverse problem to estimate source contributions and their uncertainties.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, quantitative tool for exposure source apportionment, moving beyond correlation to probabilistic attribution. It will directly inform the 'source-to-dose' component of PBT (Persistence, Bioaccumulation, Toxicity) assessments and address equifinality in exposure models by formally incorporating prior knowledge and uncertainty.
proposals.novelty: While receptor models are standard in air quality science, their application to human biomonitoring and multi-media indoor exposure assessment for organic chemicals is novel. The explicit Bayesian integration of chemical activity/fugacity concepts and kinetic partitioning data (e.g., from dust/air delay studies) as model constraints represents a significant methodological advance.

proposals.title: Inverse Source Apportionment of Bioaccumulative Organic Chemicals: A Fugacity-Based Framework Integrating Human Biomonitoring Data and Multi-Media Environmental Models
proposals.motivation: Human biomonitoring (HBM) data reveals widespread exposure to complex mixtures of organic chemicals, but linking measured body burdens to specific sources remains a major challenge. This impedes effective risk management and regulatory intervention. An inverse modeling approach is needed to quantitatively trace exposure back to key contributing chemicals and dominant emission pathways in indoor and outdoor environments.
proposals.hypothesis: By inverting a fugacity-based multi-media fate and bioaccumulation model, the relative contributions of distinct chemical sources (e.g., consumer products, building materials, industrial emissions) to observed human exposure concentrations can be quantitatively resolved, even for chemicals with kinetic delays in environmental partitioning.
proposals.method: We will develop a Bayesian inverse modeling framework. The forward model will be a dynamic, multi-compartment fugacity model, parameterized for a suite of SVOCs and informed by MacKay's prior work on kinetic delays and uncertainty. Observational constraints will include time-series HBM data (e.g., blood serum) and paired indoor/outdoor environmental concentration data. The inverse problem will be solved using Markov Chain Monte Carlo (MCMC) sampling to estimate posterior probability distributions for source emission strengths, explicitly addressing equifinality and parameter uncertainty.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, quantitative tool for regulatory and industrial scientists to prioritize source control measures based on actual human exposure. It will advance the application of fugacity models from forward prediction to diagnostic source identification, creating a direct link between HBM programs and chemical management policy.
proposals.novelty: While fugacity models are well-established for forward prediction, their inversion using HBM data to apportion sources is novel. The approach uniquely integrates kinetic partitioning constraints and a formal uncertainty framework to address the equifinality problem inherent in source attribution.

ideas.title: Source Apportionment via Chemical Fingerprinting and Bayesian Receptor Modeling
ideas.description: This research adapts the inverse problem solution of source apportionment from atmospheric science to the domain of chemical exposure assessment. The analogous problem—identifying pollution sources from ambient air concentration data—is solved using receptor models like PMF and CMB that leverage chemical fingerprints. The adapted idea develops a Bayesian receptor modeling framework for human biomonitoring and environmental concentration data. It uses known chemical signatures from potential sources (e.g., specific plastics, pesticides, dietary components) as priors to inversely solve for the key contributing chemicals and their sources, scaling from individual exposure to population-level patterns.

ideas.title: Inverse Exposure Source Apportionment for High-Priority Chemicals
ideas.description: This research direction integrates inverse modeling techniques from contaminant hydrology/air dispersion with the need for reliable exposure data on high-priority chemicals. It develops and applies source inversion algorithms specifically tailored to chemicals identified as high-risk (e.g., specific PFAS, novel flame retardants) using their unique environmental fate and transport properties. The goal is to use measured concentration data from exposure monitoring networks (e.g., in water, air, or biomonitoring) not just to quantify population exposure, but to inversely calculate the most probable geographic sources and their emission timelines. This directly informs targeted source control and remediation by identifying which specific facilities or historical releases are responsible for the observed high-priority exposures.

ideas.title: Apply Compartmental Ecosystem Modeling to Contaminant Fate in Aquifers
ideas.description: This idea adapts the solution from ecological nutrient cycling modeling to the problem of predicting organic chemical distribution in aquifers. In ecology, compartmental (multi-box) models are successfully used to track the fate of elements like nitrogen as they move and transform between defined ecosystem reservoirs (e.g., living biomass, soil organic matter, water). The analogous solution involves defining compartments, quantifying inter-compartmental transfer fluxes, and modeling the system with mass-balance differential equations. Adapted to hydrogeology, we define aquifer compartments such as the freely flowing groundwater, water in low-permeability zones, and sediment fractions with different sorption capacities. The contaminant's 'fate' is then modeled by parameterizing transfer rates for processes like sorption/desorption and biodegradation between these boxes, providing a powerful, integrated framework to predict long-term contaminant sequestration, persistence, and plume behavior that goes beyond traditional advection-dispersion models.

ideas.title: Adapting Metabolic Pathway Modeling for Aerosol Organic Carbon Aging
ideas.description: This idea proposes to solve the challenge of modeling complex organic aerosol aging processes by drawing an analogy from systems biology. In metabolic engineering, constraint-based models like Flux Balance Analysis are used to predict outcomes in complex biochemical networks. By adapting this solution, we can construct a similar network model for atmospheric organic carbon, where nodes represent chemical species (e.g., specific organic compounds, oxidation states) and edges represent transformations (e.g., oxidation, oligomerization, fragmentation). The model would incorporate known and hypothesized reactions, constrained by realistic atmospheric conditions (oxidant concentrations, actinic flux, etc.). Using linear programming, the framework would predict the dominant chemical pathways, the evolution of organic carbon fractions (e.g., from volatile to low-volatility), and how this dynamically aging matrix affects the gas-particle partitioning of persistent organic pollutants (POPs), thereby enhancing the dynamic aerosol module with a mechanistically rich, computationally tractable representation of composition.

ideas.title: A Tiered, Multi-Model Approach for Chemical Exposure Quantification
ideas.description: This research idea adapts the tiered, multi-model ensemble approach used by climate scientists (e.g., the IPCC) to the problem of quantifying exposure for high-priority chemicals. The analogous solution involves using a hierarchy of models, from simple to complex, and conducting model intercomparisons to quantify uncertainty. The adapted idea is to create a standardized framework where prioritized chemicals are assessed through a coordinated suite of exposure models. This would involve establishing a tiered model system (e.g., high-throughput screening followed by refined probabilistic modeling for top priorities) and launching a formal model intercomparison exercise. This project would generate robust, consensus-driven quantitative exposure data with clearly characterized uncertainties, directly informing regulatory risk prioritization and management.

ideas.title: Predicting Chemical Fate Using Graph Neural Networks Adapted from Drug-Target Interaction Models
ideas.description: This idea applies an analogical solution from computational pharmacology to environmental chemistry. In drug discovery, graph neural networks (GNNs) successfully predict a drug's pharmacokinetic fate (ADME) from its molecular structure. We adapt this by using similar GNN architectures, but instead of training on drug-protein interaction data, we train them on environmental chemistry datasets that link molecular structures to key fate parameters like soil adsorption coefficients, biodegradation rates, and air-water partitioning. The adapted model would learn the complex relationships between molecular substructures and environmental behavior, providing a unified, structure-based tool for predicting exposure potential and environmental fate, much like ADME models predict a drug's fate in the body.

ideas.title: Adapting Atmospheric Chemistry Data Assimilation for Groundwater Reactive Transport Models
ideas.description: This research seed expands foundational knowledge of groundwater contaminant transport and fate by drawing an analogy from atmospheric chemistry. In that field, foundational knowledge of chemical mechanisms is integrated with real-time observations through sophisticated data assimilation systems (e.g., 4D-Var) to improve air quality forecasts. The adapted idea is to develop a parallel framework for groundwater. A process-based reactive transport model, encoding current knowledge of physical and biogeochemical fate processes, would be integrated with a data assimilation engine. This hybrid system would continuously ingest multi-parameter field monitoring data to dynamically calibrate uncertain kinetic and equilibrium parameters. The outcome would be a significant improvement in predictive model accuracy and a reduction in forecast uncertainty for contaminant plume behavior, effectively translating foundational process knowledge into more reliable, data-informed predictions.

ideas.title: ML-Informed Bayesian Inversion for Contaminant Source and Fate Attribution
ideas.description: This research fuses machine learning (ML) for fate prediction with inverse modeling for source identification to create a novel, chemically intelligent inversion framework. The core innovation is using ML models, trained to predict transport and transformation parameters directly from molecular structure, to provide the necessary, chemical-specific prior distributions and likelihood functions for a Bayesian inverse solver. This allows the system to not only pinpoint a contaminant's source location and release history from sparse plume data but also to predict its future environmental distribution and potential toxicity, even for novel or poorly characterized substances. The approach transforms generic source inversion into a predictive tool for comprehensive chemical attribution and risk forecasting.

ideas.title: Dynamic Contaminant Fate Modeling Using Metabolic Pathway Analysis
ideas.description: This research challenges the standard assumption of stable chemical properties in contaminant transport models by drawing an analogy from systems biology. In metabolic engineering, dynamic Flux Balance Analysis (dFBA) successfully models how changing conditions alter metabolic pathways and fluxes. We adapt this solution to environmental engineering by conceptualizing the subsurface as a dynamic biogeochemical network. The adapted idea is to develop a constraint-based contaminant fate model where contaminants and their degradation products are nodes in a reaction network, and time-varying environmental parameters (e.g., electron acceptor availability, pH, enzyme/microbial biomass) define the kinetic constraints on reaction edges. This approach would systematically predict how the transient formation and transport of degradation products alter long-term plume behavior, moving beyond static partition coefficients and first-order decay constants.

ideas.title: Dynamic Chemical Reaction Network Mapping and Integration for Predictive Contaminant Fate Modeling
ideas.description: This research develops a closed-loop system to overcome the intractability of modeling evolving chemical properties. It first employs advanced analytical chemistry to empirically identify and prioritize critical degradation pathways and products in complex environmental matrices. These pathways are formalized into a dynamic reaction network model, which is then integrated into a reactive transport framework. The system continuously assimilates data from in-situ environmental sensors, allowing the model to update reaction rates and species concentrations in near-real-time, transforming a static assumption of stability into a dynamic, evidence-based forecasting tool.

ideas.title: A Self-Validating, Uncertainty-Quantified Tiered Framework for High-Throughput Chemical Hazard Assessment
ideas.description: This research develops a closed-loop computational assessment system that directly addresses the accuracy-reliability trade-off. It employs an active learning framework where high-throughput predictions (Tier 1) automatically flag chemicals for targeted, higher-fidelity experimental or simulation testing (Tier 2) based on prediction uncertainty, chemical similarity to known toxicants, and exposure potential. The results from Tier 2 are continuously fed back to retrain and improve the Tier 1 models. Every chemical assessment is output with a quantified confidence score and a clear delineation of the evidence base, ensuring regulatory decisions can be made with transparent understanding of prediction robustness and remaining knowledge gaps.

ideas.title: Adapting Ecological Niche Modeling for Portfolio-Based Chemical Mixture Risk
ideas.description: This idea applies solution methods from ecological niche modeling in conservation biology to the original problem of managing collective chemical exposure risk. In ecology, Species Distribution Models (SDMs) predict a species' range using environmental variables, and ensemble/covariance methods are used to assess aggregate biodiversity risk. By analogy, we can model the 'exposure distribution' of individual chemicals based on analogous variables like production volume, environmental persistence, mobility, and population proximity. These individual chemical risk models are then aggregated into a portfolio view using ensemble forecasting and covariance analysis. This adapted approach allows regulators to identify geographic or demographic 'hotspots' where exposures to multiple chemicals covary, creating a prioritized map of cumulative mixture risk, moving beyond single-chemical assessments to manage the portfolio of chemical threats systematically.

ideas.title: A Mechanistic Tiered Framework for Prioritizing and Deconstructing High-Risk Contaminant Mixtures
ideas.description: This redesigned approach establishes a closed-loop system to make mixture research tractable and actionable. It begins by using high-throughput screening and computational toxicology to prioritize individual chemicals based on potency, prevalence, and specific molecular initiating events. These are then algorithmically grouped into defined mixtures based on shared targets or pathway crosstalk, enabling focused experimental testing. The resulting mixture data feeds back to validate and refine interaction models, creating a prioritized and mechanistic research pipeline that moves efficiently from single chemicals to their most hazardous combinations.

ideas.title: Reactive Tracer Diagnostics for Engineered Attenuation of Plume-Scale Organic Contamination
ideas.description: This research direction adapts and advances reactive and partitioning interwell tracer tests (RITT/PITT) from petroleum reservoir engineering to diagnose and manage complex organic contaminant plumes in groundwater. By deploying suites of conservative and reactive tracers that mimic the transport and degradation behavior of specific contaminants (e.g., chlorinated solvents, petroleum hydrocarbons), the method quantifies the in situ degradation capacity, mass discharge, and source zone architecture. This real-time, field-scale diagnostic data is then used to calibrate and adapt remediation strategies—such as enhanced bioremediation or monitored natural attenuation—transforming plume management into an adaptive, performance-optimized process based on direct measurement of subsurface reaction kinetics and mass removal.

ideas.title: Adapting Network Pharmacology's Polypharmacology Approach to Environmental Mixtures
ideas.description: This idea adapts the solution from network pharmacology, which successfully challenged the assumption of single-target drug action by focusing on multi-target effects of drug combinations. The analogous problem in toxicology is the assumption that chemical risk can be adequately assessed by studying individual contaminants. The adapted research idea inverts the focus to mixtures by borrowing the 'polypharmacology' framework. It proposes to develop computational network models mapping mixture components to interconnected biological pathway targets, and to prioritize high-throughput screening of real-world environmental mixtures against these networks. This shift aims to identify synergistic/antagonistic interactions and emergent mixture-level toxicological outcomes that are invisible in single-chemical studies.

ideas.title: Personalized Exposomic Risk Prioritization via High-Throughput Virtual Screening of Individual Chemical Exposure Profiles
ideas.description: This research direction integrates high-throughput computational toxicology (ToxCast, QSAR, read-across) with dense, longitudinal personal exposure monitoring (wearable sensors, bio-specimens) to create a framework for personalized exposomic risk assessment. Instead of screening the entire chemical universe generically, the system prioritizes and computationally assesses only the specific mixture of chemicals detected in an individual's personal exposome. This enables the generation of a dynamic, personalized risk score that accounts for an individual's unique exposure profile and susceptibility, moving from population-level hazard identification to individual-level risk prioritization. The continuous stream of real-world exposure data also serves to iteratively refine and validate the computational models, improving their predictive power for complex, real-life mixtures.

ideas.title: Dynamic Data-Driven Prioritization (D3P): A Closed-Loop Framework for New Chemical Substances
ideas.description: This system overcomes the critical data gap for new chemicals by embedding a mandatory data-generation engine within the prioritization process itself. It employs predictive toxicology and read-across from existing chemical spaces to perform an initial, uncertainty-aware prioritization. This initial ranking then activates a tiered testing protocol, where substances flagged for higher potential concern or higher uncertainty must generate specific, predefined data sets within a set timeframe. The newly acquired experimental data is fed back into the prioritization models, continuously reducing uncertainty and improving the ranking accuracy in an iterative, adaptive cycle, ensuring the prioritization is both actionable and evidence-based.

ideas.title: Predictive Prioritization of High-Risk Transformation Products via Generative and Predictive ML
ideas.description: This research direction develops a machine learning framework that inverts the traditional chemical risk assessment pipeline. Instead of starting with parent compounds, the system first uses generative models to predict the most likely abiotic and biotic transformation pathways and products for a given chemical space. It then applies predictive ML models to rapidly estimate the environmental persistence, mobility, bioaccumulation potential, and toxicity of these virtual transformation products. The core innovation is the closed-loop, generative-predictive prioritization, which identifies high-risk chemical transformation products that warrant monitoring and regulatory scrutiny before they are synthesized or released, fundamentally shifting risk management from reactive to pre-emptive.

ideas.title: Educational Portfolio Risk Simulator for Chemical Mixture Fate and Prioritization
ideas.description: This initiative develops an open-access, simulation-based educational tool where learners manage a 'portfolio' of organic contaminants. Users apply financial portfolio risk techniques to prioritize chemicals based on their simulated environmental fate, transport, and transformation, making complex systems analysis intuitive and engaging. The platform captures anonymized user decisions and model inputs, creating a large dataset on perceived and calculated risks of chemical mixtures from a diverse user base. This synergy both trains the next generation of environmental chemists in critical systems-thinking skills and produces a novel, crowd-informed dataset to validate and improve real-world chemical prioritization frameworks.

ideas.title: An Adaptive Surveillance and Predictive Prioritization Framework for Emerging Organic Groundwater Contaminants
ideas.description: This research develops a proactive, data-driven system to manage the risk from evolving suites of organic groundwater contaminants. It establishes a national-scale groundwater surveillance network using non-targeted analytical chemistry to detect and identify novel chemical signatures. These detections feed into predictive models that estimate compound mobility, persistence, and toxicological hazard to generate a dynamic risk priority list. The output is an adaptive management protocol that guides regulatory attention and remediation strategies towards emerging threats before they become widespread, fundamentally shifting the paradigm from reactive to anticipatory groundwater protection.

ideas.title: Adapting High-Throughput Phenotypic Screening from Drug Discovery for Chemical Prioritization
ideas.description: The original idea challenges the infeasibility of assessing all environmental chemicals with traditional methods. An analogous problem exists in pharmaceutical drug discovery, which overcame the bottleneck of testing vast chemical libraries by adopting high-throughput phenotypic screening (HTS). This solution used automated, cell- or organism-based assays to rapidly identify bioactive compounds. Adapted to environmental science, this involves developing a tiered assessment framework where thousands of chemicals are first screened through a battery of high-throughput, high-content bioassays modeling critical toxicity pathways. The resulting phenotypic data would serve as a rapid, cost-effective prioritization filter, directing intensive assessment resources only to those chemicals showing biological activity, thereby making the systematic evaluation of large chemical inventories more feasible.

ideas.title: Prioritise transformation products over parent compounds using a metabolic pathway screening approach
ideas.description: This idea inverts the focus from parent compounds to their transformation products by drawing an analogy from pharmacology. In drug development, the solution to the problem of potentially active or toxic metabolites was to proactively screen for them using metabolic pathway models (both in vitro and in silico) early in the pipeline. Adapted to environmental chemistry, this means developing analogous 'environmental metabolic' screening systems. The core research idea is to create standardized platforms—such as high-throughput photolytic, hydrolytic, and microbial reaction chambers coupled with advanced analytics and computational prediction—to generate, identify, and prioritize the major transformation products of chemicals of commerce. The goal is to shift risk assessment from a reactive stance on parent compounds to a proactive, predictive prioritization of transformation products, which may pose equal or greater risk, by treating environmental degradation as a predictable metabolic process to be mapped and screened.

ideas.title: Prioritise by Intrinsic Hazard: A 'Dark Matter' Approach to Chemical Risk
ideas.description: This idea adapts a core problem-solving method from astrophysics to the challenge of chemical prioritisation. In cosmology, dark matter cannot be observed directly, so scientists prioritise its study based on the potency and nature of its gravitational effects on visible structures. Similarly, for chemical risk, we can relax the crippling assumption that precise exposure data is a prerequisite for action. The adapted solution is to first systematically prioritise chemicals based on a robust assessment of their intrinsic hazard and biological potency—their 'effect field.' This involves leveraging high-throughput in vitro and in silico data on fundamental mechanisms (e.g., receptor binding, reactivity) to rank chemicals by their potential to cause harm, explicitly assuming a background state of ubiquitous, low-level exposure. This creates a hazard-centric priority list to guide targeted exposure assessment and monitoring, effectively searching for the 'chemical dark matter' with the strongest gravitational pull on biological systems.

ideas.title: Dynamic, Risk-Informed Monitoring Network Design for Emerging Chemical Threats
ideas.description: This research synthesizes chemical prioritization and monitoring network design to develop a dynamic strategy for environmental surveillance. It first applies reviewed prioritization schemes to rank the hundreds of new substances introduced annually based on their toxicity, mobility, persistence, and potential use. The output risk scores and chemical properties (e.g., degradation rates, sorption potential) then serve as critical inputs to a dual-objective optimization model that determines the optimal number, placement, and sampling frequency of monitoring wells. The result is a cost-effective, evolving monitoring network that is proactively reconfigured to detect the plumes of the highest-risk emerging chemicals, transforming monitoring from a reactive, generic practice into a targeted, early-warning system.

ideas.title: Microfluidic aquifer chips with integrated chemical sensors for reactive transport visualization
ideas.description: This research expands the original microfluidic aquifer model concept by drawing an analogy from biomedical microfluidics, specifically organ-on-a-chip technology. In that field, a key solution for studying dynamic processes is the integration of optical chemical sensors directly into the microdevice to provide real-time, spatially resolved data on biochemical conditions. Adapting this solution, the proposed research involves creating lab-scale microfluidic chips that simulate aquifer pore structures and are fabricated with integrated chemical sensor materials (e.g., for pH, specific ions, or contaminants) within their porous geometry. This adaptation enables the direct visual study of reactive contaminant transport processes—such as adsorption, precipitation, or biodegradation—at the pore scale, offering a powerful tool to visualize and quantify the coupling between physical flow and geochemical reactions in subsurface environments.

ideas.title: A Mechanistically-Grouped, Observation-Constrained Framework for Multi-POP Transport Modeling
ideas.description: This redesigned research system moves beyond a simple parameter swap by developing a chemistry-first framework for simulating diverse POPs. It begins by grouping compounds (e.g., pesticides, PBDEs) based on shared degradation pathways and partitioning mechanisms, then builds tailored sub-models for each group within the larger GEM architecture. The core innovation is an iterative constraint loop where preliminary model outputs are rigorously tested against region-specific monitoring data and controlled perturbation scenarios, allowing for the identification and correction of compound-specific process representations before global application. This results in a more robust, transparent, and empirically validated tool for assessing the global fate of multiple POP classes.

ideas.title: Personalized Risk Mitigation Feasibility: Assessing the Limits of Exposome-Tailored Remediation
ideas.description: This research direction investigates the technical and economic feasibility of eliminating personal health risks from organic contaminants by grounding the analysis in high-resolution, individual-level exposome data. It shifts the challenge of remediation feasibility from a generic, environmental compartment question to a personalized one, asking what level of risk reduction is possible given an individual's unique and dynamic exposure profile. The synergy enables the development of personalized 'risk elimination feasibility scores' that account for the totality of an individual's exposures, the irremediable background burden, and the cost-benefit of targeting specific exposure pathways. This provides a realistic, data-driven framework for individuals and clinicians to prioritize interventions with the highest probability of meaningful risk reduction, acknowledging the limits of perfect safety.

ideas.title: Inversion-Driven Smart Tracer System for Subsurface Contaminant Fate Mapping
ideas.description: This approach designs a new class of environmentally-deployable 'smart tracers' whose physical transport and chemical transformation are exquisitely sensitive to specific subsurface conditions (e.g., redox potential, specific surface area). A minimal, targeted injection of these tracers into the contaminant pathway provides a high-resolution signal. This signal is processed through an adaptive geostatistical inversion model, co-developed with the tracer chemistry, which does not just locate the contaminant but quantitatively infers the hidden hydrogeological and biogeochemical parameters controlling its movement and degradation. The result is a closed-loop diagnostic system that maps the subsurface's functional architecture to predict contaminant fate and optimize remediation strategies without extensive invasive sampling.

ideas.title: Constraining Architectural Uncertainty to Ground Contaminant Fate Predictions
ideas.description: This research program confronts the fundamental limitation that predictive models fail due to unknown subsurface heterogeneity, not just incomplete process knowledge. It develops an integrated framework that identifies the critical architectural data needed to usefully apply process models at field scales. The work combines targeted laboratory studies on fate processes with the development of novel sensing and inversion methods to cost-effectively map key heterogeneities. The outcome is a practical, site-adaptable methodology that quantifies and reduces prediction uncertainty by directly linking essential characterization efforts to the most sensitive parameters in transport and fate models.

ideas.title: Closed-loop active learning system integrating predictive modeling with automated experimentation for chemical fate and exposure
ideas.description: This system creates a self-improving research pipeline that directly tackles data scarcity. Machine learning models trained on existing data identify the most uncertain and impactful predictions for novel chemicals or environmental scenarios. These candidates are automatically prioritized for experimental testing using robotic high-throughput screening platforms that measure key fate and exposure parameters. The new, high-quality data is then fed back to retrain and improve the models, creating a continuous cycle of hypothesis-driven experimentation and model refinement specifically designed to expand the model's reliable applicability domain.
