ideas.title: A Global Chemical Inventory Prioritization Framework Using a Biodiversity Conservation Triage Model
ideas.description: This idea applies an analogical solution from biodiversity conservation to the problem of scaling up chemical prioritization. In conservation, the IUCN Red List provides a globally standardized triage system to prioritize thousands of species for protection efforts based on evidence-based threat categories. Adapted to chemical inventories, the research would involve developing and comparing a similar tiered prioritization framework. Chemicals on global inventories (like TSCA, REACH, etc.) would be systematically categorized into priority tiers using a consistent set of criteria—such as hazard properties, exposure potential, and use patterns—enabling efficient, scalable identification of substances requiring urgent regulatory attention or further study among thousands of candidates.

ideas.title: The Contaminant Fate Challenge Platform: Gamifying Environmental Chemistry Pathways
ideas.description: Drawing an analogy from astrophysics citizen science projects like Galaxy Zoo, this idea adapts the solution of gamified, accessible platforms to spark interest in organic chemical environmental fate. The analogous field solved engagement by turning massive data analysis into simple, rewarding tasks with educational scaffolding. The adapted research idea is to create 'The Contaminant Fate Challenge Platform,' an interactive educational initiative where users engage with virtual environments to predict the fate of organic contaminants. By completing gamified challenges—such as tracing a pesticide's journey from soil to groundwater or predicting a pharmaceutical's degradation in a river—participants learn core concepts of sorption, biodegradation, and transport. This hands-on, discovery-based approach serves as a compelling entry point, motivating deeper study and potentially recruiting new scientists into the field of environmental organic chemistry through an engaging, low-barrier-to-entry experience that demonstrates the field's relevance and intellectual puzzles.

ideas.title: Uncertainty-Aware Global Chemical Prioritization with Integrated Data Confidence Tiers
ideas.description: This research develops a global chemical inventory prioritization system that explicitly accounts for data quality and uncertainty. Instead of applying schemes directly to raw inventory data, it first classifies all available data points (e.g., hazard, use, exposure) into confidence tiers based on source reliability, measurement vs. estimation, and completeness. The core prioritization algorithm then calculates probabilistic priority scores and confidence intervals, not single-point rankings. The final output is a stratified list that distinguishes substances identified as high-priority with high confidence from those where the designation is highly uncertain due to critical data gaps, thereby making the prioritization actionable for risk management and transparent for further data gathering.

ideas.title: AOP-Informed Mixture Prioritization Framework Using Uncertainty-Aware Portfolio Logic
ideas.description: This framework adapts the conceptual structure of portfolio diversification—balancing known risks against unknowns—to chemical mixture assessment without requiring invalid quantitative aggregation. It uses Adverse Outcome Pathway (AOP) networks to group chemicals by shared mechanistic targets, identifying potential for interactive effects where co-exposure is likely. The system then applies a prioritization logic that diversifies regulatory and testing resources across different 'uncertainty buckets,' reducing overall model uncertainty about collective risk rather than optimizing a falsely precise risk estimate. This creates a robust, evidence-guided strategy for managing chemical mixtures under conditions of profound data limitation.

ideas.title: Adapting Atmospheric Trace Gas Lidar for Subsurface Contaminant Plume Mapping
ideas.description: This idea adapts the solution to the analogous problem of mapping atmospheric trace gas plumes (solved using Differential Absorption Lidar - DIAL) to the context of groundwater contamination. In atmospheric science, airborne DIAL systems use two laser wavelengths to create 3D concentration maps of gases like methane by measuring differential absorption of backscattered light. The adapted research idea proposes developing and deploying a similar airborne DIAL system, but tuned to the specific infrared absorption signatures of volatile groundwater contaminants (e.g., chlorinated solvents). By flying over a contaminated site and measuring the subtle differential absorption of laser light reflected from the ground, which is modulated by contaminants diffusing through the unsaturated zone, the method would generate a high-resolution map of the spatial distribution and concentration gradients of the subsurface plume, offering a rapid, non-invasive complement to traditional well-based monitoring.

ideas.title: Adaptive Monitoring and Management Framework for Evolving Organic Contaminant Plumes
ideas.description: This research develops a novel, integrated framework that couples the optimal design of groundwater monitoring networks with the adaptive management of organic contaminant plumes. The core innovation is a dynamic feedback loop where data from a strategically placed, cost-minimized sensor network is continuously used to update predictive models of plume migration and degradation. These updated models then inform real-time adjustments to remediation strategies (e.g., injection points, rates) and can trigger re-optimization of the monitoring network itself as the plume evolves. This synergy moves beyond static monitoring or management to create a responsive, cost-effective system that minimizes long-term environmental and human health risks from complex, spreading groundwater contaminants.

ideas.title: A Dynamic Evidence-Integration Framework for Context-Specific Chemical Prioritization
ideas.description: This research develops and validates an open-source computational framework that dynamically prioritizes chemicals for human health concern based on continuous evidence synthesis. The system integrates diverse data streams—including exposure potential, hazard severity, population susceptibility, and evidence strength—to generate actionable, scenario-based priority rankings rather than a single static list. It is designed to be updated in near-real-time, transparently weights different evidence types, and allows stakeholders to adjust parameters for specific regulatory or public health contexts, thereby directly supporting targeted risk management actions.

ideas.title: Probabilistic Regional Contaminant Fate Models with Adaptive Data Assimilation for Confidence-Aware Risk Assessment
ideas.description: This research develops a new generation of regional contaminant fate models that explicitly treat and reduce critical uncertainties. The core system integrates hydrology, land use, and chemistry within a probabilistic framework that assimilates heterogeneous, sparse observational data to continuously update parameter distributions and model states. It produces not just a single risk prediction, but spatially explicit confidence maps that highlight areas where predictions are robust versus highly uncertain. This confidence-aware output directly informs adaptive monitoring strategies and supports more transparent, defensible regional environmental management decisions under uncertainty.

ideas.title: A Data Assimilation Framework for Contaminant Transport Using a Digital Twin Approach
ideas.description: This research idea proposes to solve the challenge of integrating field and lab data with simulations by drawing an analogy from Numerical Weather Prediction (NWP). In NWP, data assimilation techniques like the Ensemble Kalman Filter are used to continuously integrate vast streams of observational data into complex physics-based models, creating a dynamically updated and more accurate forecast system. Adapted to contaminant hydrology, this approach involves establishing a 'digital twin' simulation of a contaminated site. Field sensor networks would provide ongoing observational data, which is assimilated into the transport model in near-real-time. This framework statistically optimizes model parameters and states, reducing uncertainty and creating a self-correcting, high-fidelity representation of real-world contaminant behavior for improved management and forecasting.

ideas.title: A Modular, Multi-Resolution Framework for Regional Contaminant Fate Modeling
ideas.description: This research adapts the modular, multi-resolution simulation frameworks pioneered in computational astrophysics and cosmology to the challenge of scaling up contaminant fate models. The analogous problem—modeling physics from galactic scales down to stellar processes—was solved using techniques like Adaptive Mesh Refinement (AMR) and the coupling of distinct physical modules (gravity, hydrodynamics, etc.) within a single computational framework. The adapted idea is to develop a large-scale regional model for contaminant fate and risk assessment that integrates hydrology, land use, and chemistry not as a monolithic system, but as a flexible, hierarchical simulation. This framework would employ a core regional model at a coarse resolution, dynamically zooming in with finer mesh grids on areas of high risk or complexity, while using scientifically robust parameterizations to represent sub-grid processes. This approach balances computational feasibility with the necessary detail for accurate regional-scale predictions.

ideas.title: Epidemiological Assessment of Remediation Efficacy and Residual Risk
ideas.description: This research direction applies epidemiological study designs (e.g., cohort, case-control) to populations living on or near historically contaminated sites that have undergone remediation. It aims to determine if post-remediation health outcomes (e.g., cancer incidence, birth outcomes) differ from unexposed control populations, thereby providing direct evidence for or against the feasibility of complete risk elimination. This approach shifts the question from a technical/economic debate to an evidence-based public health evaluation, linking remediation actions directly to population health metrics. The results would create a powerful evidence base to inform chemical prioritization for remediation and set realistic expectations for risk management outcomes.

ideas.title: Adapting Astronomical Signal Detection Pipelines for Chemical Prioritization
ideas.description: This research idea proposes to systematically apply reviewed chemical prioritization schemes to the several hundred new substances added annually by adapting solutions from astronomy. In astronomy, automated pipelines for transient surveys (e.g., ZTF) use multi-tiered, machine-learning filters to prioritize a flood of new celestial detections for valuable telescope time. We adapt this by creating a computational prioritization pipeline for new chemicals. The pipeline would implement a sequence of automated filters and models, each encoding a different reviewed prioritization scheme (e.g., for persistence, bioaccumulation, toxicity). New substances would flow through this pipeline, receiving scores at each stage, culminating in a consolidated, ranked priority list. This automated, tiered approach efficiently directs limited resources for deeper assessment and testing towards the most potentially hazardous or concerning new substances, mirroring how astronomers allocate telescope time to the most promising astrophysical candidates.

ideas.title: Adaptive Threat Detection: A Dynamic Feedback Loop for Chemical Risk Research
ideas.description: This system uses initial prioritisation to direct foundational research but embeds a mandatory, continuous challenge mechanism. A parallel stream of broad, non-targeted environmental and biomonitoring data is systematically analyzed for unexpected signals or trends. When significant anomalies are detected—such as a novel compound or a spike in a low-priority substance—they trigger a targeted reassessment and can dynamically reallocate a portion of research resources. This creates a learning system that not only investigates known priorities but also actively discovers and escalates emerging risks, preventing blind spots inherent in static priority lists.

ideas.title: Applying the 'Residual Risk' Framework from Infectious Disease Control to Organic Contaminant Remediation
ideas.description: This research seed expands by drawing an analogy from infectious disease control, where the goal is often 'elimination' (local zero incidence) rather than global 'eradication,' acknowledging persistent residual risk. The analogous solution involves managing pathogens to an 'acceptable' level through surveillance and sustained intervention. Adapted to organic contaminant remediation, this research would challenge the technical and economic feasibility of absolute risk elimination. It would develop a framework to define 'acceptable residual risk' thresholds for remediated sites, integrating analytical detection limits, contaminant bioavailability, long-term environmental fate, and cost-benefit analysis. The study aims to shift remediation goals from unattainable complete elimination to pragmatic, sustainable risk management that includes post-remediation monitoring and contingency plans, thereby providing a more scientifically honest and economically viable model for environmental cleanup.

ideas.title: Adaptive Epidemiological Testing: A Sequential Design for Cost-Effective Chemical Hazard Identification
ideas.description: This research develops a sequential, adaptive testing framework that begins by applying epidemiological designs (e.g., case-control, cohort) to population-level biomonitoring and health data to generate initial, probabilistic rankings of chemical-health outcome associations. The methodology then incorporates an optimization algorithm to determine the minimal set of targeted, confirmatory tests (e.g., in vitro or targeted animal studies) required to upgrade these observational signals to a pre-specified confidence level for prioritization. This creates a closed-loop system where each round of minimal testing informs the refinement of the epidemiological model, which in turn guides the next most efficient testing cycle. The result is a prioritization pipeline that maximizes the use of existing real-world data to drastically reduce the cost, time, and animal use of traditional toxicity testing while providing statistically confident rankings for regulatory or research action.

ideas.title: Adaptive Portfolio Management for Compound Risk Research
ideas.description: This idea adapts the portfolio management approach from finance to direct research on prioritised compounds. In finance, capital is allocated to assets based on a dynamic risk-return analysis. Analogously, a central 'research portfolio' is established for high-risk compounds identified by prioritisation schemes. Experimental and monitoring resources (e.g., lab capacity, funding, field work) are then strategically allocated in proportion to each compound's priority score and the urgency of its data gaps. The portfolio is managed dynamically, with regular reviews to shift efforts towards the most critical, emerging risks and away from topics where sufficient knowledge has been acquired, ensuring optimal use of limited research capacity.

ideas.title: Engineered Microbial Consortia for In Situ Bioremediation of Complex Organic Groundwater Plumes
ideas.description: This research idea adapts solutions from human gut microbiome engineering to improve the management of broadly distributed organic groundwater contaminants. In gut health, complex, engineered microbial consortia are used to perform multi-step metabolic processes to restore system function. The adapted idea is to develop new bioremediation strategies by designing and deploying purpose-built microbial consortia into contaminated aquifers. These consortia would be engineered for syntrophy, where different microbial members work in sequence to fully degrade complex mixtures of pollutants. The approach would include tailored nutrient or 'prebiotic' amendments to selectively support the engineered community, enhance its longevity, and drive it to outcompete indigenous, less effective microbes, leading to more reliable and comprehensive in-situ cleanup of diffuse organic chemical plumes.

ideas.title: Robust monitoring network design under source and hydrogeological uncertainty
ideas.description: This research develops a stochastic optimization framework for groundwater monitoring network design that explicitly accounts for uncertainty in contaminant source characteristics and subsurface heterogeneity. The method generates a large ensemble of plausible release scenarios and geological realizations to simulate potential plume evolution. It then formulates an optimization problem that minimizes cost while maximizing a robust detection metric, such as the expected probability of early detection across all scenarios. The resulting network design is therefore resilient to the unknowns inherent in real-world contamination events.

ideas.title: Hybrid Remote Sensing and Autonomous Ground Sensor Fusion for 3D Groundwater Plume Characterization
ideas.description: This redesigned approach overcomes the surface-blindness of traditional remote sensing by creating a closed-loop, multi-modal detection system. Satellite and aerial data first identify surface anomalies indicative of potential subsurface contamination. These targets then guide the deployment of a dense, adaptive network of in-situ groundwater sensors that provide direct vertical and horizontal concentration measurements. A data fusion engine combines the broad spatial coverage of remote sensing with the precise, depth-resolved chemical data from the sensor network to generate accurate, three-dimensional models of contaminant plumes, significantly improving detection reliability and mapping fidelity for hidden groundwater pollution.

ideas.title: Predictive Personal Exposome Intelligence for Pre-emptive Risk Mitigation
ideas.description: This redesigned system moves beyond reactive exposure assessment by creating a predictive framework. It fuses real-time personal sensor data with spatiotemporal forecasts of environmental hazards (e.g., air pollution plumes, pollen counts, UV index) and individual biological susceptibility markers. A machine learning engine uses this integrated data stream to model an individual's likely future exposures and predicted biological response, generating personalized, actionable alerts and mitigation recommendations before high-risk exposures occur. The goal is to shift the paradigm from documenting the exposome to intelligently navigating it.

ideas.title: Adversarial Validation Loop for Contaminant Fate Prediction
ideas.description: This research creates a closed-loop system that integrates field data, lab experiments, and simulation through an adversarial framework. Instead of one-way calibration, the simulation is continuously challenged by targeted field observations designed to expose its weaknesses, particularly in heterogeneous and poorly characterized environments. The system uses these discrepancies to drive adaptive sampling and refined experimentation, dynamically updating the model to reduce epistemic uncertainty. This results in a more robust and self-correcting predictive tool that explicitly accounts for and learns from real-world complexity.

ideas.title: Seismic Back-Projection for Contaminant Source Inversion
ideas.description: This research adapts the seismic back-projection technique from seismology to solve the inverse problem of contaminant source identification. In the analogous field, seismic networks back-project recorded waves to image an earthquake's rupture. Here, a sensor network's contaminant concentration time-series data is treated as a wavefield. The solution involves developing an algorithm to time-reverse this plume data, propagate it backward through an appropriate fluid transport model (e.g., advection-diffusion), and coherently stack the signals on a spatiotemporal grid. The point of maximum stacked 'concentration energy' reveals the most probable source location and its release timeline, providing a robust, model-informed inversion method for environmental forensics.

ideas.title: Optimal Sensor Placement for Contaminant Detection via Compressive Sensing Principles
ideas.description: This idea expands the original monitoring network design problem by drawing an analogy from compressive sensing in signal processing. The analogous problem—reconstructing a sparse signal from very few measurements—is solved by designing measurement matrices that preserve essential information. Adapted to hydrogeology, the contaminant plume is modeled as a spatially sparse field. The research will formulate the optimal well placement as the design of a sensing matrix that satisfies geologically constrained properties analogous to the RIP. The goal is to create an optimization framework that selects well locations to maximize the information gain for plume characterization (solving the detection/estimation dual) while explicitly minimizing installation and monitoring costs, leveraging sparsity-promoting reconstruction techniques like L1-regularization to guide the network design.
